Most of my learning came from taking the support class (highly recommended!), going to the learning center, and using outside resources like the organic chemistry as a second language textbook. The exams are difficult, but the curve is very generous

analysis.name: Martin Graef
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 16 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: An overall great professor. He explains concepts clearly and is very kind and approachable. I took him for an upper-level biology course, and exams were mostly based on lecture slides and recorded lectures. If you keep up with the material, the tests were very fair. Office hours were extremely helpful, even though not many students attended.

Martin was an excellent professor, clearly very knowledgeable and approachable during office hours. Lectures are fast-paced if you take handwritten notes, but still very engaging and fascinating. Grading is extremely fair, as exams are all MCQs and directly relate to lecture content. TAs are wonderful - take BIOMG1350 even if bio isn't your thing.

He is clearly passionate about Biology, but lectures can be fast-paced if taking handwritten notes, and kind of monotonous. Nevertheless, always accessible by OH and super knowledgable. Prelims are probably the fairest you can get--straight from lectures/slides, MCQ, do well on them and maybe skip the final. Easy class, memorization heavy!

Uninspiring to say the least. One of those classes that you take to get it over with. Nothing wrong with him but just reads off the slides. Voice could put me to sleep. Do well on the prelims and you can skip the final though.

Martin really cares about the students' learning and success. He is passionate about his research and cell biology in general. The exams were very fair, and the questions were directly from the lectures.

analysis.name: Kyle Harms
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 79 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: The majority of my friend group dropped this class, and I should have followed suit. The write-ups for the projects are genuinely unbearable and seem infinite. The course is listed as a web design course, but it feels like an essay-based class. Good luck getting help on Ed, where Prof. Harms response with the vaguest answers on earth. Do not take.

Without a doubt, this is by far the worst class and professor I've ever had at Cornell. He treats his students like children (not in a caring way but a patronizing one) and acts like a toddler whenever you try to talk to him. He may appear nice and act as if he cares but he's inflexible and will forsake your wellbeing for his own. Be warned.

This class is abysmal, honestly one of the worst classes I have taken at Cornell. While it may seem like a web dev class at first, the class is much more focused on unnecessary, busy-work writing than actual coding. For the first project in this class, I coded around 1-200 lines, but had to write over 3000 words to describe my "design process."

I was excited to learn about web dev at an esteemed institution such as Cornell. Kyle deserves no esteem, nor is his class a worthwhile use of time, unless you like doing arts and crafts, since all projects incorporate endless design drawing portions, which kill both trees and students' sanity. I also wrote more in this class than I did for my FWS.

INFO 2300 is a very easy class, but the thing is that Harms is just really not understanding of any extraneous situation and is really inflexible. I do not recommend this class if you have some kind of coding experience and it's not required because the web design contents covered in INFO 2300 can easily be learned over just one or two days.

analysis.name: Melissa Hines
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 171 reviews, this is a reliable picture

review_highlights.comment: 2070 has historically been a bad class. Melissa did not make the class any more enjoyable. She was rude to students and the lab TAs. The lecture content was not similar to prelims and the lab reports were incredibly disorganized. If you have to take a class from her I would recommend waiting to take it from someone else.

Please do not take M Hines's class. I took it for CHEM 2070, and I fear it was extremely difficult. She sped through the lectures incredibly quickly, and she made the prelim questions so hard. She also wears the same maroon wool sweater, black pants, and cargo shoes. She does lore drop about her days at MIT, though, which was fun to hear.

Not a bad lecturer but I disliked the class. In multiple instances, she hasn't taught us a topic yet but expects to understand how to solve it in the group quizzes, and then proceeds to teach the material the day the group quiz is due. She also gave practice exams without formula sheets where you literally could not solve without the given data.

Lectures and Learning Catalytics are critically important to understand and grasp the concepts and ideas. Group Quizzes are a little more challenging but help you to master these concepts. Prelims are tricky but everything you need to know is either said by her or written as notes. Only a few TAs are helpful. Review notes daily and practice.

Hines is bad. What angers me the most is that she doesn't bother to provide formula sheets ahead of the exam when half of the practice exams posted online require knowing what values and equations were given. This created a huge pain in the butt, and her group quizzes are ridiculously long and you better pray for competent group members.

analysis.name: TJ Hinrichs
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: unclear grading rubric with weekly quizzes
She is super nice and smart! I enjoyed her class. Thank you, Prof. Hinrichs!

Course was heavily based on Historiography, not History. Lectures are optional after the 2 in-class quizzes, but section is mandatory. Very essay heavy, with some kind of paper being due weekly. Reading everything assigned would kill you; just read Lewis and Sima Qian and you'll survive. Professor seems to care about students, which is a plus.

Very down-to-earth and genuinely cares about her students. Sit-down discussions are very interesting but slide lectures can be a little dull. She is super supportive outside of class for a STEM student like me who is not familiar with college history courses. Would definitely take again.

TJ is really nice and she definitely cares about her students. But, her class has a unique structure that is really difficult to get used to. There is a large amount of reading to do. You discuss the readings and questions in groups and then present to everyone. Very confusing and hard essay questions. She asks you to define everything. Participate

analysis.name: Sylvia Hristakeva
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 13 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: I had a 94 in the class, 95% on the final, 94% on the midterm, and 100% on all of the assignments. This fm gave me a 20% for my participation grade, which dropped my grade to 89.9%, and I'm pretty sure she did it on purpose. DO NOT take this professor.

Prof Hristakeva's marketing class was more challenging than I thought it would be. Overall, she is a good prof but the exams were graded very harshly. Participation and attendance matter a lot - more than most other courses at Cornell.

Lectures were mandatory, violently boring, with seating charts, name tags, and no laptops allowed. Despite having to do four group projects, three individual homework assignments, and participate in lecture, 70 percent of the grade for this class was based off of the exams, which are primarily memorization focused (closed book).

DO NOT TAKE! Wait one semester! She requires mandatory attendance and makes you wear name tags and assigned seating as if we are in middle school. She treats her students like children it is honestly extremely off putting. Attendance based on participation in class despite it being a 100 person lecture. Also bad accent and so boring during lecture!

I enjoyed the material quite a lot given my curiosity to see how marketing drives business outcomes. However, I feel like we are evaluated less on conceptual understanding but more on the jargons and hard-coded definitions for them, which I feel loses a sense of practicality and applicability for the principles.

analysis.name: Hadas Ritz
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 126 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Class seems to be going well at the start then all of the sudden she throws a bunch of material and assignment at us at a time that we were supposed to be on break. The material that she threw at us was not easy and was not explained at all in lecture, she simply just clicked through the slides without a care in the world for her students.

Ritz isn't the worst, but her grading with Learning Objectives (LO) is. You could be doing good all year, getting 3/3s on LOs on all 4 graded quizzes, and going into the final with 14/21 graded LOs as 3/3s and then bam. If you don't get the answer almost perfectly right you get a 1/3 and your grade is ruined. It's perfection or nothing with grades.

The new grading system is... interesting. I (and many others) did not like Ritz's course (the lectures move painfully slow), and it covers shockingly little material (no transforms??). The grading scheme doesn't aid with "learn at your own pace" and instead obfuscates what should be a pretty simple class. The material's easy, but grading isn't.

I think her teaching is fine, but most of her responses to questions on Ed Discussion are snarky and unfriendly. She will refuse to give actual answers to genuine questions and respond with things along the lines of "I will not entertain this" or "Why are you asking this?". It just makes me lose respect for her as a person.

Really clear lectures with helpful lecture notes. Not too much homework. The "outcome-based" grading system (look it up) is innovative and really rewards student effort. This professor got a bad reputation a few years ago, but has improved a ton since then. I would highly recommend taking math with them.

analysis.name: Donna Haeger
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 23 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Mrs. Haegar was dedicated to ensuring her students not only learned Excel but also enjoyed the process. This year placed a strong emphasis on AI, and her enthusiasm for what the technology is capable of made it clear how passionate she is about teaching. I recommend introducing yourself to her and the TAs who help make this course such a success.

Originally, I was nervous about this class after reading the reviews. However, she is very kind, caring, and willing to explain any questions you have about the course. Her prelims were fair and a clear representation of what was taught in class. As long as you respect class time and are on top of your work, you will do fine.

She is nice to students during lectures, and also like to help students on their HW. The HWs can be a little hard since they aren't always exactly the same as in the demos, and the HW instructions can be unclear. The beginning of the semester was a bit confusing, and the prelims are a little hard and very time consuming. She teaches very fast.

I oved Professor Haeger's class and as an instructor, she did a good job. What I vehemently disliked was the disrespect towards students. I had her for an early class period, so students often would arrive a little late. Each time she would stop the whole class to essentially berate the student in front of their peers. It was absolutely unnecessary
Grade is based off whether or not you have a good TA. Some are stricter than others. Prelims are unorganized, but manageable if you watch her videos.

analysis.name: Ivanna Yi
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 6 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: There really isn't a lot of work for this class and the midterm was fairly easy. A big chunk of your grade comes from participation and projects.
I've heard poor things about this professor and how little effort she puts into making the class fair. It is definitely true. Do not take class.
Quite disrespectful to students. Had a disappointing experience similar with others.
Does not listen to students, very selfish professor. You will see.

Group projects are fun and engaging. The lecture itself is also interesting. Very respectful and open to students' opinions and thoughts. Assignments are not too heavy. If you at least read and pay attention, no problem at all. Highly recommended class!

analysis.name: Joseph Halpern
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 33 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Nothing about this class makes sense, the lectures are rushed and incomprehensible for even simple topics, the homework is graded excessively harshly, and attendance is somehow mandatory despite lecture having no benefit to the students. I believe I'm on track for a good grade, but in retrospect it this class made it as hard as possible to learn.

Seems he might have been better at some point in the past but every single lecture in this course was just rambling while putting a different professors (textbook) slides on the projector and typing math in plaintext on them :( . By contrast Prof. Eva Tardos who is co teaching this semester does a fantastic job of writing and working out examples.

He's changed a lot recently, unfortunately for the worse. He's completely incoherent in lectures and very difficult to follow along given that he just mumbles off the slides with little explanation or visuals. The lecture hall is always filled with confused students. One time when he asked who was confused, half the students raised their hands.

He would just read off his lecture slides and not really have anything interesting or useful to say. Lectures were barely helpful for homeworks and tests. I relied mostly on the textbook and OH to learn in this class since lectures taught nothing.

he cares about the content and the field, but he should not teach. he mumbles all through the lectures, its extremely hard to keep up with what he is saying and he just makes the already hard course even more impossible to pass. Kids stoped coming to lectures even though attendance was madatory cause that's how bad it was.

analysis.name: Immanuel Trummer
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 20 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Class is eh. People are right that it is 'easy', the hard part is parsing the content out of the lecture material. Slides are very long, while what is important is not always evident. Exams have a strong emphasis on SQL which for a DB class I think is good, but I found them difficult. SQL is covered but not enforced to the degree which is tested.

This class is relatively interesting, but takes SO MUCH time. Only take it if you have a significant interest in getting started with DB design. You will need equally committed partners.
If you choose to take it take advantage of opportunities to fix and ask for regrades. Additionally, make sure to rigorously test.

He might be good at researching, but he is absolutely not good at lecturing and breaking things down. If you take his class, be ready to learn on your own for more than 80% of the time. Even many CS lovers on YouTube outperform him in terms of teaching DB stuff.

This class was terrible. Legit could win an award for how badly he teaches. The content isn't necessarily hard, but his extreme lack of explanation and his terribly worded problem sets make it unnecessarily difficult. To top it off, the course staff he hires for this class have absolutely no idea what they are doing and mess up when they help.
The content of the course is really useful. However, his lecture is hard to understand even with the video recordings from past semester.

analysis.name: Daniel Lorey II
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 20 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: I took this class for the CALS requirement, as did most students likely. It was not a class to sleep on (lots of memorization required) but material is just above that of an advanced high school class. Prof. Lorey has thorough slides and breaks down concepts in class with on-the-fly notes. Attendance optional, but I'd go. Class is curved at the end

The course content is not for the weak, but Dr. Lorey explains it in a way that makes it easy to understand. I dreaded missing his class because one lecture could mean missing important details not explained deeply in the textbook. The exams reflected the prelim reviews, and his lectures really helped make sense of the material.
Clear, consistent, and actually cares for his students. Dr. Lorey will get you through Chem1560 as long as you are willing to put in the work.
THE MOST BORING LECTURES EVER YOU WILL FALL ASLEEP,  The way he teaches is so boring and make you go to sleep. Every time I'm there I fall asleep, not only me but many students

The class starts out pretty easy, but after the first prelim, picks up speed pretty quickly. Dr. Lorey is a great professor, if something isn't clear to you during class, hes good for in class questions and provides plenty of office hours for you to get help.

analysis.name: Tristan Ivory
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: He's super knowledgable and a good guy to talk to but his content in WLC tends to be all over the place without as much cohesiveness as other lecturers. In regards to the exam def the toughest to prepare for b/c you have to cite readings in a closed-book exam. Exam Qs were rough but grading seemed more lenient relative to other profs

Had him as the second professor for Work Labor Capital. He mixed lectures with documentaries which I enjoyed. Provided a list of all of the readings on the final which was useful as we were required to quote from them during the short answer section. Final was much harder than Boyer's so beware of the difficulty spike. A decent prof.
He mixed lectures with documentaries which was interesting but the test was hard. Short essay and short answers, a couple people weren't able to finish. DO THE READINGS and take notes.

Great professor. Readings were insightful and informative. He expertly covers a plethora of interesting topics, including workplace cultures and discrimination. Papers are really easy (just have to reflect on the readings), and tests were graded fairly. I enjoyed the lectures, but if you aren't interested in the topics they may be boring. Gives EC.

Class itself was not that difficult just incredibly boring and required you to do an obnoxious amount of reading which was NEVER talked about in class so you had to infer meanings on the tests. Tests are mostly short answers and discussions were useless. He seemed like he did not want to teach the class and word-for-word read off the slides.

analysis.name: Ian Hewson
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 7 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Prof. Hewson is amazing! I was nervous going into this class because I have no background in oceanography, but it's been super interesting and not stressful. Learn a lot of content but no tests/quizzes. He often ties his own research/field work into class content and you can tell he just wants to get students excited to learn!
am a senior and this is one of the best classes i have taken because it intersects microbiology, marine biology, and eeb. very very interesting course

Prof. Hewson is clearly very knowledgeable&passionate about his field, but the lectures are very dense and got to be a bit monotonous at the end of the semester Luckily there was no exams on the detailed lecture content, rather theres weekly literature/writing HW, group proj, and final proj. This was nice but made it too easy to tune out lecture

This class was really interesting. The prof went above and beyond to make the class accessible and flexible in the online learning environment. While the lectures were dense, learning goals were clearly identified and the assessments were firmly focused around these. Also assessment was flexible and tailored to each student's preferred method.
This was a great introduction to marine biology. Some of the labs were a little disorganized but at least there were labs!

analysis.name: Ileen DeVault
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 53 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Professor DeVault is one of my favorite professors in ILR. She is extremely knowledgeable and cares a lot about your learning. The readings were worth it and she always has her door open to students if you ever want to discuss essays, ideas or topics covered in class. Attend lecture for good grades!

Devault was the most biased Prof I had at Cornell. Teaches 'historical facts' but implies clear political ideology. Grading scheme (letter grade averaged out at the end based on the lowest range of that letter) doesn't make sense. Lectures dull, didn't encourage diverse discussion. Decently easy class though.

No clear grading criteria. Essay grade ranges were insane and many students had conflicting thoughts. Slides have pictures not actual content. Of you don't go to lecture, you're done for. We also had a final which is unnecessary. Also, did I mention the class was electronic free?  If I had to do it again, I probably wouldn't take it with her.

DeVault is one of the nicest people I have ever met. She genuinely cares about her students. I had a class during her office hours, so she regularly scheduled other times to meet. I struggled a lot with the first essay assignment, but DeVault helped me and taught me what I did wrong. Her lectures can be dry, but the information is interesting.

Awesome professor, super passionate and nice.  Lots of weekly reading with reading quizzes in discussion, but you can definitely "skim" main concepts and do extremely well. Two papers, go to TA office hours and you will do well on them. Extremely fair final. Some find the subject boring overall but DeVault makes it as painless as possible.

analysis.name: Jim West
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Professor West is a only a decent lecturer, but he is VERY good at explaining things when you ASK QUESTIONS. To succeed, sit at the front, ask questions, and attend office hours! Professor West is available very often, so take advantage! He loves to get to know students during his office hours, and is more than willing to accommodate your schedule.

His lectures were often unclear and difficult to follow, leaving many students disengaged. Attendance expectations felt random and inconsistent, adding to the lack of structure. I frequently relied on outside resources to learn material that should have been covered in the lecture.

Every kid in his class deserves a refund on their semester's tuition. His lectures were so useless that maybe a fifth of the class attended. On the upside he knew his lectures were bad, so the tests were easy. Also the homework solutions are online. Using the MIT lectures on YouTube gives an easy A.

Jim West is very kind and intelligent, but I fear that since he was been an expert in "Geometric topology and infinite-dimensional topology" for so long, he no longer really is able to teach undergraduate classes. That means he usually is unable to explain the material in a way that someone new to the material can really understand it.
Lectures boring and not very well attended. Good textbook though and he let us know which sections each lecture/homework covered. No website and unclear grading guidelines. Not great not horrible.

analysis.name: Jason Mokhtarian
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Funny and always accessible. A lot of readings but only the parts discussed during class are covered. Clearly cares about his students and always available for unscheduled office hours. Unfortunately, he's sometimes unprepared and can't answer some basic questions. Midterm graded very reasonably.

Every day in class, you do the same boring activity. He is a lazy unmotivated faculty member who created the curriculum years ago and has never once revised it. He would come to class having not reviewed anything related to the material. Additionally, he provides 0 feedback on your work and doesn't explain why he takes points off.
Genuinely one of the laziest professors I have had. Every class was exactly the same and he just liked to listen to himself talk. He was often condescending and grading was completely arbitrary.

Professor Mokhtarian is an incredible professor who cares deeply about seeing his students learn and succeed. The assignments are beneficial to your understanding of the information and never feel unnecessary. He will push you and your writing skills, but it is 100% worth it and you will be proud of your assignments by the end of the semester.

This class is fascinating, and even students with a Jewish education background will learn a ton. There's a lot of reading and writing involved, which can hit hard in the middle of the semester when it's 10 degrees outside. He is accessible outside of class and encourages students to participate.

analysis.name: Itai Cohen
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 38 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: It was the most disorganized class ever. The course with over 100 students only has one TA, with office hours once a week. Post no review for prelim, and never respond on ed discussion. Poorly organized class and professor can't teach.

This might be the worst class I have taken at Cornell. The class is disorganised, nobody responds on the ed, he takes a month to respond to emails, and nobody (students or TAs) has the answer key to practice prelims. It genuinely feels like he doesn't want his students to succeed in this class. Lectures are confusing and hard to follow along with.
The class is so catastrophically taught that I wanted to drop out of physics altogether. Honestly so sad that we get terrible professors even though we pay so much in tuition
Don't bother taking this class if you want to learn anything. The prelims are far too difficult for how terribly the professor teaches us the content.

Horrible organization of this class. First class in all of my engineering career that does not provide answer key to the practice prelims so you don't even know if what you are doing is wrong. Not enough TAs to help you. No one answers to my ED discussion posts other than other students who are trying to also understand what's going on.

analysis.name: Julie Laudenschlager
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 13 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: She is the lab director for General Chemistry. The TAs for CHEM 2071 are expected to deduct a set number of points (with quotas), and students have been discouraged from requesting regrades. Rubrics are not provided before lab reports are due and are instead created afterward, with TAs instructed to grade very harshly. Nice person, however.

She makes the lab reports difficult because we are not allowed to see a rubric, and her grading is very inconsistent. She grades super strictly and somehow expects you to read her mind. Also, if too many students submit regrade requests, she will go into lecture and yells at everyone. She genuinely prays on every student's downfall.

She makes it unnecessarily difficult to do well on labs by changing the grading rubric each time and taking off points for the smallest things. She expects students to read her mind and know exactly what to include in lab reports (e.g. which outside sources to cite in the introduction). This course was honestly more difficult than CHEM 2070 lecture

Does not respond to emails and will not release rubrics so students are left guessing what they did right and what they should keep on doing for following labs. Lab TA's are don't have them either and are left confused with grading as they also don't know rubrics till after we submit. Graded on things that were never asked of us in instructions.

Does not provide rubric for labs to students or TAs, so there is no way to improve performance. Grades on things never mentioned before, then tells students not to submit regrades. Leaves comments on reports that is not constructive, but critical and unsupportive.

analysis.name: Justin Murfin
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 6 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: He's obviously a brilliant guy, but he needs to do a better job of making sure everyone in the class understands what he's talking about. He's not great at going over the basics, and the pre-reqs for the class underestimate the course content. If you're working on an S&T/Fixed Income desk, this is the class for you. If not, you're out of luck.
The two reviews on Jan 20 are very likely his own ratings, given the name-calling "Dyson kids" and the clarifications. Decided to drop as the office hour policy discourages us from seeking help.

Pretty straightforward with his class, and he aims to help you understand the content if you have questions. However, his office hours can sometimes be inconvenient, so it can be hard to reach out to him. He doesn't really publish anything on canvas, and his lecture notes are pretty disorganized. It can start to get confusing if you skip lectures.

The professor is smart and excellent in his field. However, the teaching is hard, and office hours, or even after class inquires, are almost impossible. The professor is always busy with things and doesn't even have a minute to answer students' questions. Exam questions are vague, and there are dozens of formulas you have to memorize, all tough

He is terrible at breaking things down and he expects you to understand the content well. You cannot stay to work through a problem set in his office hours because "it's not his job to guide you through it" but rather answer very specific questions. The assignments and exams were very vague as he is more focused on his research and academic drama.

analysis.name: Justine Modica
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 11 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Dr. Modica is genuinely kind and energetic. However, she is an impossible professor to have. She is obviously an expert in her field, but I think she carries a mindset that her students are equally expert, assigning 200+ pages of reading at times. Her Final Exam is essentially impossible unless you study 12+ hours/day. TA Findley is very cool tho.
She knows her stuff, exams are tough, and she cares a lot. She's super pretty, so it's a bonus if u get bored with the lecture. Findley the TA is the goat.

People are being harsh. Lectures are informative, she's very passionate about the course/her students. Definitely a difficult course but also the readings can be easily skimmed, and the writing assignments like Kheel Center/Film review are interesting/grade boosters. TA is super chill. Final seems really difficult though, may change in future.

Really nice person. Only a couple of assignments, 2 Exams. 30% of the class is participation. Very long readings and a very hard final exam. Class will require a lot of studying, but you will learn a ton of labor history. What I appreciate most about Dr. Modica is that she's open to change in how she formats readings, assignments, and exams.

She has a lot on the lecture slides and expects everyone to be familiar with what's on the slide and in the readings. The hardest part is that she doesn't make it clear what will realistically come up again in the course; best piece of advice is go to office hours as she tends to be a lot more clear there.

analysis.name: Hyun-ho Joo
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 3 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Very simple format; grade is made up of attendance, weekly reflections (graded on completion), a midterm essay, and a take home final essay. Kind professor, really cares about the material. Not much to prepare for class other than doing the readings. Interesting and low-stakes class

Prof was so kind and cared so much about the material. Class was a mix of lecture and discussion, he really wants people to participate. Lectures basically reiterate the readings, which aren't too heavy. Not many assignments in the class, and prompts are given in advance so you can prepare your essays ahead of time.

In an hour and a half, Prof. Joo's lectures accomplish what could otherwise be achieved in three sentences. He has good intentions and the readings are interesting, but the class itself mostly consisted of him reading excerpts of what we were already expected to read.

analysis.name: Philipp Kircher
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 11 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Such a difficult class BECAUSE HE MAKES IT THAT WAY! Get your ECON 3030 credit somewhere else because this class genuinely is so difficult for no reason. If you care about your GPA, don't take this class. He has extremely difficult content in problem sets (6 a semester), after lecture quizzes, and most of the time he couldn't finish his lecture.

His class IS difficult. the PSs are pretty long but good practice for the tests. He really cares for his students and WILL try his best to help you if you reach out, and he's genuinely passionate about what he teaches. To get good grades, make sure you understand the concepts and do the sections and really listen to the lectures.

This class is genuinely brutal. Just one of those classes you need to get through for Econ major but it's frustrating the entire time. P-sets are actually absurd and the entire course is multi variable calculus despite half the class not having taken that class. Prof seems like a good dude but it doesn't really matter when the class is unreasonable

He's good at explaining things in lecture. He's caring and always willing to answer questions during class and after. It's a tough class and problem sets are long but the work is helpful for exam prep. If you have to take this class, I would recommend taking his!

Sweet professor who cares about course and students, but very difficult course. P-sets would take upwards of 8 hours each (6 p-sets for the course and one drop). Exams are much easier than p-sets. Lectures are difficult to follow, but posts lecture slides. Accessible for OH. Would recommend, but be ready to put in a lot of work outside of lectures

analysis.name: Katrina Nousek
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Her grading criteria was very unclear and she didn't give us good feedback on how to improve our writing. I would say that my AP Lit/Comp class taught me like 5x more about how to write well, than this class did. It's unfortunate because it means that we are not making good use of our tuition/time/energy here at Cornell.

If you choose to take a class by this professor... don't. If you have to take a class from this professor... good luck. She is hard to listen to and rants for ages. The class has no clear rubrics or standards for writing. She grades harshly with little to no room for improvement no matter how much time you may spend. Save yourself, please.

Such an interesting class and the professor is clearly well informed (rambles a bit too much). Only issue is she barely gives out As and doesn't give proper feedback. Super nice during office hours and overall a great teacher.

analysis.name: Khena Swallow
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 23 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Swallow's class has potential to be much more interesting than it actually is; she's passionate about the material but lacks any enthusiasm in teaching resulting in boring lectures. Class is very easy though since 1 prelim gets dropped completely, and you can make up participation on piazza. Overall recommend.
Horrible class with busy work and a horrible, disorganized professor
Very dry professor with a very disorganized course

She is a bit disorganized in her lectures and it can be hard to follow. Her tests mainly surround the slideshows but I would use the textbook as extra information. She does test you on the location of parts of the brain that can be challenging. It is possible to do well. You would just have to do most of the learning on your own and extra credit!

Probably the most boring professor I have ever listened to. Complete and total waste of 27.5 hours of my life that I would do anything to get back. First lecture she mentions how taking notes by hand is more useful but she doesn't even give enough time to type half of the slide down. Asks what you think, talks about everyone's answer and goes on.

analysis.name: Leah Perlmutter
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 6 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: I felt like whenever she did lectures instead of Matt I always would have to review notes since the lectures were harder to follow. Although she was very engaging, I felt like she tried to hard to bring it outside topics into the course that made us run out of time to focus on what were expected to actually learn

You can tell she's really trying her best, but her lectures are harder to follow and feel less content-dense compared to Professor Eichhorn. Went on tangents sometimes about non-related course material when there wasn't even enough time allotted to cover the actual course content. Hope she's able to hit her stride and shoutout Xi'an street food!

Leah teaches college students like an elementary school teacher. She will wait till everyone stops talking, spends most of the lecture talking about inclusivity, and rushes through the overall material. Avoid her at all costs.

Great lectures and great lecture notes. Really supportive professor who is very willing to help outside of class. Provides practice prelims very similar to real ones. However, homework assignments are long and very harshly graded.
Lectures were really disorganized, hard to follow, and unprepared. Exams were mostly easy enough to compensate. Homeworks took a lot of work and didn't really feel that beneficial for learning.

analysis.name: Lawrence Shuster
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Prof Shuster is patient if you don't understand a topic. He will take the time to thoroughly review theory concepts. In my experience, if you really want to understand music theory, both 2101 and 2102 are essentials at Cornell. My musicianship has greatly improved since taking these classes. Puts the fun in fundamentals in my personal opinion.

Not to invalidate anyone else's experience, but I personally didn't see any of the concerning behaviors reported in the other reviews. He seemed like just a well-intentioned guy, perhaps with a strange fascination on Chinese martial arts (and some concerning YouTube recommended videos). Lectures were a bit unclear at times, but grading was lenient.

prof shuster loves to cold call and make targeted and passive aggressive comments when students are struggling with the material. he seems to insist it's all very easy even though it is in fact quite difficult at times and he doesn't really explain most of the material. he's also very disorganized and made some weird comments about autism and women

This class was incredibly disorganized (no syllabus) and fast-paced. Students weren't given adequate materials to be successful; there are no class notes, instead he writes on a chalkboard and cold calls with questions about information that has not yet been taught. The professor picks on students who do and don't know the answer.

Extremely disrespectful - especially towards women. Made a joke that a student who was majoring in Philosophy and Music had parents who were disappointed by her. She cried. Passive aggressive in email as well as in person.

analysis.name: Vikram Krishnamurthy
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 43 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Vikram is an amazing prof if you are looking for a mentor who pushes research like a company CEO and is into publishing paper like a product. He extensively manages his students keeping track how much time they spent on research etc and if the money is being utilised well for the time you spend on research. If you like that style, you will love him

The best sophomore class at Cornell I have taken. Prof Krishnamurthy is inspirational in the class. He went out of the way to  explain the assignments in the office hours. I strongly recommend this course as an ENGRD requirement. The assignments are difficult and time consuming but u learn a lot. Prof. K really knows his stuff.

(FA2021) He spent a lot of time on how very basic calculus he would re-teach (a course prerequisite) is graduate material and that the course material was invaluable for interviews. He taught algorithms by putting code onto slides in one giant PDF. Generous grader, but stressful since you need to rely on Google to complete homework.

I did this class in spring 2024 and enjoyed it a lot. He is a very smart guy, and he is very caring too. ECE 2720 is offered both semesters but the instructor changes each semester. One of the best ENGRD courses I have done

This is an excellent and mathematically heavily rigorous course. I am a CS-major.  I did not know that linear algebra had so many real world applications. This guy seriously rocks, he is very approachable. The assignment take a lot of time. But no prelims :)  One of the best courses at Cornell by far. Final exam will be take-home.

analysis.name: Cecilia Lawless
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 47 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: I'm so traumatized by this professor that I've come back ten years later to share my experience. In fall 2016, I was extremely depressed, and she took that personally. She would often call me into her office and berate me like she was my mother. I always left sobbing. I wish I could go back and stand up for myself. It's just a class.

Someone else said this and I agree (took last semester): "A very eccentric professor, wants the class to be free and open but grades harshly when you do not specifically respond to the ridiculous questions that occur to her when reading your writing. I'm not sure how I feel about her she's okay but I would not recommend taking a class with her."

Cecilia is a very odd instructor and it was obvious that very few in our class trusted her. She does the bare minimum and never provides any feedback but gets upset if you ask about grades. Quite obvious to everyone that she just wants to come to class without being prepared for the lecture. I'd rate her teaching skills as very poor.

A very eccentric professor, wants the class to be free and open but grades harshly when you do not specifically respond to the ridiculous questions that occur to her when reading your writing. I'm not sure how I feel about her she's okay but I would not recommend taking a class with her. Also she never gave grades so my final grade was a surprise.

She takes a bit of getting used to. She is a bit disorganized and never knows what time it is, and her assignment directions can be a bit confusing. However, she is very goodhearted and genuinely tries to help students if they tell her they are having trouble. She is passionate about Spanish and indigenous peoples. Class helped my Spanish so much!!

analysis.name: Kyle Lancaster
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 54 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: He's the best. He loves to blow things up and is the best and most entertaining teacher! His explosive personality really makes the class interesting and bearable. 15/10 would recommend.

Dr. Lancaster is, hands down, one of the best professors you'll ever have in any Chemistry class at Cornell. He is always clear and explains complicated concepts in a simple way while managing to entertain the class at the same time. Always willing to help students in office hours and was a DJ at one point in time.

The GOAT of all Gen Chem Classes. Lancaster was funny, and understood how much students typically dislike the subject, so he tried to make it as enjoyable as possible. His exams were tough, but his final compensated for that heavily. Would take his class again just to experience his in-class demonstrations for the first time. Definitely tenured.

Super knowledgable guy, very engaging lectures. Homework assignments are long and challenging but very well-written and overall enjoyable. Available both during stated office hours and at other times, and by email.

If there is one class I could retake at Cornell, it is CHEM 2070: General Chemistry I with Dr. Lancaster. Not only is he hilarious and a chemistry legend, but he also has deep respect for his students. He actually wants to know his students and help them achieve their goals. I couldn't have had a better intro to Cornell and chemistry thanks to him.

analysis.name: Michael Lynn
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 47 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Lynn is very defensive and somewhat aggressive towards students. He posts announcements with wording like "I have already told you, but here it is again" and "apparently, some of you interpreted my previous email as saying I do not want to meet with you. Not true." He borderline yells when you ask questions he doesn't have a concrete answer to.

It seems like he has been living under a rock for the last decade because he has no idea about technology or social media marketing. We watch videos from the 80s and pretend like theyre still relevant. Doesnt know simple math. No point in going to lecture except for iclicker because he just reads verbatim off his Comic Sans PowerPoint

He is the bottom of the barrel in every sense of the phrase. Material is based solely on his own beliefs and never presents counterarguments. When students question the material, he belittles them, even though he is the one who's definitively demented. Rude and pretentious. Blatantly sexist. Complete disgrace to the Hotel School. Do not enroll.

Avoid if you can. The lectures are hyper-specific, obscure, and contradictory. The only way to prep for the exams - memorize (despite what the prof says).

The prof himself is very rude to students. I don't understand why be a prof if you don't like or know how to teach.

Save your tuition money.

Despite success in the class, worst professor I have had at Cornell. Exams are all T/F about random and obscure concept from lecture/book. Is very rude and makes inappropriate comments in class about race / gender. Contradictory. rude when students reach out and rude when score low or students are confused. Outdated concepts. Offers 0 assistance.

analysis.name: Matthew Paskell
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: He's awesome. I took him last semester for bioethics and he made the class really enjoyable. Creates a great environment for discussion of arguments and objections, too, which is helpful for a philosophy course. Just make sure you do the readings (there are occasional pop quizzes) and ask questions when necessary. Would recommend.

Good FWS professor! Had him for Bioethics and he was very chill and made discussions engaging. Super down-to-earth and nice. Gives good feedbacks on essays and readings are minimal. Beware of occasional popquizzes in class, but they're not hard and worth very little. Not a harsh grader--do the readings, participate, and you'll be fine.

I had Professor Paskell for my FWS Bioethics. Super interesting class, and he always made discussions very interesting! There were usually readings for each class, but most of the time very short (and very minimal compared to other writing seminars). Also gave very good essay feedback (although he is a slow grader). Overall great professor.

This was for an FWS on Luck and Morality. There were a lot of readings, quizzes, and small assignments, and attendance was mandatory, so the class took a decent amount of time. However, he made the class discussions pretty interesting and gave really helpful feedback on the essays, so the class was worth taking.

Paskell was a pretty chill professor. Five essays in total, with each being weighted more as the semester progresses. Attendance in lectures and discussions were mandatory, but going is helpful just to gain further clarification on the readings and insights to write the essays.

analysis.name: Mark Sarvary
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 53 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: I would second the disorganized comment. Lectures too seem useless.

He teaches pointless information in 9am lectures which is later covered during 3-hour, unskippable labs. Getting an A+ in his class is just about impossible, and lecture attendance can be done remotely(most days). When the course is helpful, it's not directly due to his teaching, but because of the lab TAs. His teaching needs more direction.

The class itself is quite trivial if you have any experience in biology, but an easy A. Sarvary's lectures are quite fun, just make sure to do the PollEv for attendance. The labs (and getting out of them early) heavily depend on the TA, but generally really straightforward and easy. Very flexible assignment schedule.

Sarvary is really nice and his lectures are chill and enjoyable, but ultimately the lab TAs are a lot more important in shaping your experience with the class. Grading is annoying, as an A+ is nearly impossible (99.00+), and my grad TA was really stingy with points. Sarvary is great and deadlines are flexible, but be ready for boring 3-hour labs.

These other reviews must be lying because BIOG1500 is not a great course. It insists upon itself and is a tad bit pretentious. Nothing against the professor, it's just that the course is a lot of busy work and not very informative.

analysis.name: Matthew Wilkens
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 19 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Professor Wilkens is one of the best professors I've ever had. He is incredibly engaging and makes the content that he is teaching so enjoyable. He is accessible outside of class, and shows care for each and every one of his students. He wants his students to learn and enjoy learning at the same time. Without a doubt best professor at Cornell.

No curve. Cares more about the TAs than the actual students. Boring lecturer. Truly the worst experience ever with a professor. The final was uncharacteristically hard and he really doesn't seem to care about the student. He shouldn't teach this course!

Hands down one of the best professors I have had at Cornell, and that was in his first semester here and during the pandemic. He did a great job balancing the CIS side of the course with the implications/impact in the humanities. He was extremely caring, made sure the teaching staff was flexible, and actively sought out and implemented feedback.

Professor Wilkens is indubitably the most caring and passionate professor I have met. His lectures are engaging, and he genuinely cares about students' understanding. The grade was based on a semester-long project, 8 problem sets, a midterm, and a final. The workload can be challenging, but Professor Wilkens and his TAs will help you succeed.

Both the midterm and the final were very similar to the practice tests, so everything felt very fair. He clearly puts effort into making the course run smoothly and explains the material well. The homework was lengthy, but it helped reinforce the concepts. Overall, he is a strong instructor and I learned a lot from the class.

analysis.name: Tejasvi Nagaraja
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 20 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Honestly, this is an amazing class. Take it. It has a pretty simple format: one midterm, two essays, and some in-class assignments/ quizzes. He also gave an option for extra credit. Every assignment allows you to explore what YOU are interested in. He is so flexible,open to connecting with students beyond the classroom, so passionate. Trust me-TAKE

Honestly he is amazing. This course you get to explore so much of what you are interested in. I wish he explored more Latin history. But he is so much fun, love the music he plays in class and the topics we discuss. Really dynamic format. Super great. Easy if you try on assignments. Trust!.
Great guy, boring lectures. your grade relies on your TA.

Lectures were all over the place, he YAPPS about irrelevant information 80% of the class. We had 6-7 different books to read almost at random across the semester. Papers are extremely long for an intro class, and even longer readings. Extremely boring lectures, only survived because of my TA! (They are 100% responsible for all the grading).

All papers. Your grade lives and dies by the TA. This semester, there was a probably a 1.5 gpa spread between the two TAs and nothing was done about it. Your grade is about as meritocratic as a roulette table. Lectures are circular, directionless, and unnecessary. Just read the pertinent readings a week before the papers are due.

analysis.name: Robert Newman
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 18 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: If you expect to learn real-world project management skills, you won't find them here. Prelims are bizarre and harsh. You must show up before 8 AM for every lecture, phones and laptops banned, or this jerk will destroy your grades. This is the worst instructor I've had since middle school and I strongly recommend him resign from this position.

This class is a joke. You drag yourself to an 8am for zero useful info—just nonsense rants and breakdowns. He warns against "amdalga breakdowns" but literally has them himself. Tests are garbage and make no sense. Haven't learned a single thing. Absolute waste of time.

Would not recommend! The amount of work assigned is going to be way more compared to other classes with similar credit levels, and the lectures are not engaging enough for how early they are.  Slides and homework have typos, and you don't need to take this class to learn what is taught.

Self-centered and arrogant and treats his students like children while rarely offering insightful answers to genuinely meaningful questions. For a class so centered on emotional intelligence, I think he could benefit more from taking this course himself than any student enrolled currently. This course is a waste of time and money.

It takes a special type of arrogance to believe you deserve the undivided attention of 50+ people from 8-10AM. He practices what he preaches, seems to care about students. A bajillion assignments going on at all times. In a huge group all sem, felt ridiculous begging adults to study for a test because my grade depended on theirs (group average).

analysis.name: Nick Salvato
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 14 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: He was one of the best professor I could have asked for to introduce me to the PMA department.

Nick is perhaps the best lecturer at Cornell - he doesn't hesitate, break, or stutter through entire classes. He also remembers students' names within weeks in large lectures. He's also one of the clearest critical thinkers on campus. Not an easy grader, but definitely fair

Professor Salvato is one of the most compassionate professors I have encountered at Cornell. He is a great lecturer and passionate about PMA, which makes his class very enjoyable. He also pays great attention to how his students are doing as a whole, making constant efforts to check in. If the opportunity presents itself, take one of his classes!

I took Television with him (AMST 2160) and he was such a great teacher, the class clapped for him after the first and last lecture. We watched films throughout the lectures and talked about them. We were also assigned to watch these for homework and sometimes they'd be a little long. Essay grading was a little tough but doable if you put in effort

Lectures were fine, but he likes to hear himself talk, I'd reccomend going and paying attention, but this class didn't require that much work. It just wasn't as interesting as I hoped it would be. Essays are fairly light and the HW isn't bad, just keep up with it and put a little more effort than you might expect.

analysis.name: Kristoffer Nimark
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 15 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: I was very excited about the material but became very frustrated. Professor Nimark was a very harsh grader on the exams and never gave us any feedback. Grading was very inconsistent across different students. No incentive to attend class as he brought 0 enthusiasm and effort to his lectures. Said he'd “curve the class” and yet there was no curve.

Content presented in a boring, uninspiring way. Good 4000 level econ class if you don't want to overwork yourself - no required attendance, exam material can be found on the slides. His teaching methods reflects that he doesn't think having a super difficult class is necessary - which can be seen as time-wasting to some and low-stress to others.
seriously the worst lecturer i have ever had. trails off at the end of every sentence and barely looks up from his screen. also very reluctant to answer questions

This guy straight up doesnt care. Very uninspiring lecturer, shows up late from time to time and doesnt help at all. This is a very easy class from a material stand point, so I would only take it if you want an easy 4000-level course. However, I have never met someone who cares less about teaching in my time at Cornell.

The TA grades the exam, class curves to a B+, grading is arbitrary. He is not all that passionate about teaching, he reads from the PowerPoints, which are taken from the textbook. READ the Textbook, and DO the review questions, as for HW talk to the TA, not an easy A, but doable.

analysis.name: Ted O'Donoghue
analysis.verdict: Tough course, but students consistently rate the teaching highly, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 31 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Professor O'Donoghue is an outstanding professor, and his lectures were always engaging. Homework and exams were challenging, but if you focus on understanding the intuition behind the concepts, you should be fine. Lastly, his passion for the material clearly comes across in class, which made learning the material enjoyable and thought-provoking.

This is one of the most interesting and rewarding classes I have taken in the econ major. O'Donoghue is truly at the forefront of this field and often cites his own research which is fascinating. Very difficult course, but lectures are amazing and p-sets and exams are fair if you are keeping up. Would highly recommend for all majors.

O'Donoghue is kind, knowledgeable, and engaging. Behavioral economics is not an easy course, but O'Donoghue articulates concepts clearly and provides lots of materials to help. Highly recommend! Just make sure your schedule isn't too overwhelming when you add 4660 - there's a lot of material.

Extremely organized and handled the logistics of this 5-professor course. Super clear in explaining concepts and satisfyingly concise slides. I had no prior econ education but still managed to understand everything. Also helpful during office hours: an incredibly intelligent professor!

DO NOT TAKE THIS CLASS. AEM 2020/PSYCH 2940 was so difficult for no reason. Prelims and the final are 100% of your grade and the tests are extremely hard. For a class that does not even count towards your major, it is not worth it.

analysis.name: George Orlov
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 54 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Horrible lecturer, overcomplicates very simple topics to the point where you have no idea what he's talking about. Posts slides onto canvas but they do not help. Get ready to read the entire textbook if you want to know what's going on. Also assigns way too many useless homework assignments a week (50+ questions), and the prelims are difficult.

It can be a confusing course, and in Orlov's lectures, you need to pay attention to little things and the interaction between topics. You can miss up to 6 classes without losing attendance points, but sometimes it takes a while for lecture/discussion slides and lecture recordings to be uploaded. Two midterms and a final based on practice prelims.

He was my lab instructor and miscalculated my total grade back in 2015, which became a source of long-lasting frustration for me. 

I still remember how proud he seemed of himself, and how he carried that unnecessary arrogance during lab sessions. 

I feel justified making this comment now because I am currently a PhD candidate.

PollEv was kinda annoying, but other than that, he was a super chill prof who had some fun references in his exams. His lectures really told you everything you needed to know about the exams, and the slides were my best friend. One or two things seemed subjective on exams, however.

Orlov gets a lot of unjustified hate. As long as you actually go to class, the material is super understandable. He genuinely cares about making sure students know the content and his slides are very thorough. He also provides plenty of extra resources and ample help hours for extra practice. The tests are exactly like the practice exams.

analysis.name: Marie Ozanne
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: I had her at 8:40 a.m. in Spring '25. Honestly, she has so much more energy in the morning compared to her students. If you have any concerns, like problems with a groupmate or something you do not understand, she will take the time to help and explain. The prelim is straightforward if you read carefully and do the practice test.

Prof Ozanne cares about what she teaches and makes every class interesting, even at 8:40 in the morning. The grading is fair, and the prelims are pretty straightforward if you put in the time to study. She's also super approachable and helped me figure out what kind of internships to look for. Would definitely take her again.

You can tell that she is very passionate about F&B. Her prelims were all really fair. Everything she told us to study for (during our review lecture) was on the prelim and nothing more. There was a slight problem with wording in a few of her questions but other than that her class is very doable.
Great person, great teacher. I was not interested in food and beverage when I came into this class. Left the class somewhat interested in it. Nothing but great things to say about her
she cares about her students and she really likes the subject. however, she's french...

analysis.name: Peggy Odom-Reed
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 21 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Professor Reed is an outstanding professor. She is very knowledgeable in her field. Be willing to put in the work. Show effort, commitment, and discipline. Don't be afraid of making mistake.  Get out of your comfort zone and take initiative. I highly recommend Professor Reed to anyone.

This woman made me cry 3 times in one week. She is unrelenting. Even though we are in a pandemic, and she can't even make it to class due to her health issues, she expects her students to treat this class like their only obligation. She has no empathy for others. She does not give positive comments. She does not give A's. She's the worst professor.

This class is impossible to do well in. Only take it if you like to cry. Prepare to have your spirit decimated, your motivation demolished, and your will to live diminished. Turn around time is so fast on assignments that you quite literally will either have to fail all your other classes or take the L. Dont do it. Unless you hate yourself.

I genuinely wonder if she is aware that we take multiple other classes. Atrocious, merciless, insufferable, mental-breakdown-inducing, brutal, cruel, unrelenting, unendurable, overwhelming. It is absolutely, definitely, and wholly impossible to see a good grade after pulling enough all nighters to age me 50 years. I have disintegrated.
Im a recovering alcoholic and this woman made me break my sobriety amongst a puddle of tears.

analysis.name: Katelin Olson
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 8 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Took the Sustain Preservation certificate series though eCornell and Katelin was so supportive and encouraging. I haven't been in the classroom for a long time, and she cheered me on and helped me work through my analysis of a passion project. Wish she taught more courses! Highly recommend

I recently took Katelin's Sustainable Preservation certificate series through eCornell and it was fantastic. She is so supportive and encouraging and the other people in the class were amazing to connect with through her online zoom weekly sessions. Wish she would offer another class so I could take that too!
I took Dr. Olson's online Sustainable Preservation series but that's not an option to select. She was great, very approachable, knowledgeable and provided substantive feedback.

Worst Professor I have had by far in the CRP department. Grading was unfair, never returned in a timely manner, and harsh. Lectures were profusely dense with the containment of far too much information, as well as extremely fast-paced because she writes down a script of what to say. Exams and papers were difficult, lengthy, and lacked correlation.
Required course for first years in CRP. Provided a lot of information and was excited about the work. Helped me out of class improve my writing. Always available for an appointment.

analysis.name: Thomas Pepinsky
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 31 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Professor Pepinsky is an excellent lecturer, and one of the best in the government department here at Cornell. His lectures are clear and organized, focusing on a particular theme or subject. Just be aware that he doesn't post his lecture slides on Canvas. Exams are fair, and students are allowed to bring a cheat sheet the size of an index card.

GOVT 3443 is not an "easy" class to put on your schedule. It covers a lot of content and comes down to basically two prelims and a final paper. Attendance is "not mandatory," but slides are not posted, so if you don't show up you will not do good in this class. If you find this topic interesting, go for it. It's fun, deep, and very interesting.

Pepinsky is the goat, and this was probably my favorite class taken so far. Lectures are very engaging and well structured, and the content is interesting. You can easily tell Pepinsky loves what he teaches and is very welcoming in class and office hours. A lot of readings but not any more than most other govt classes. Highly recommend :)

Professor Pepinsky clearly cares about the subject and ensuring that his students succeed. VERY knowledgeable. Fair warning, he is extremely lecture-heavy, and slides are not posted after class. Though attendance is not required, it is necessary if you want an A range grade. Lecture, reading, and section material are all fair game on exams.

Honestly, Pepinsky is pretty rude towards his students. I felt it was highly inappropriate when he screamed at students in front of a large lecture hall. (Stop taking my reviews down Pepinsky)
Class was not hard, did well. Just many better government teachers who are far more engaging and kind.

analysis.name: Trent Preszler
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 23 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Preszler is a professor who thinks his class is more important than anything else in your life. He heavily picks favorites, gives little grading criteria then grades extremely harshly without giving a rubric back, and his prelims are hard for no reason (and he doesn't teach you the content in class). He makes me want to go into investment banking

Let me put in caveperson speak so clear. Much read. Professor talk many word, say nothing. Sassy. Condescending. Self-center. Grade tough for fun. TA ridiculous. Guest speaker come. Professor no care. Me no care. Give test on thing never teach. Exam long. Time short. Me still confused. Me learn by self. Professor bald. That all.

His foundations class is an absolute joke. All Dyson freshmen are required to take this course and he grades way too harshly for someone who doesn't even teach. The class is just guest speakers who come in every class (aside from prelim days).

He doesn't teach the class. Loved the guest lectures but if he's gonna have prelims in this class, they need to be based off guest lectures, not an external reading that makes no sense. Grading is so vague. Got a 60 on the first prelim and 80 on the 2nd one. This class should go back to being pass/fail. Students skipped anyway and chatted prelim.
Very dependent on exams. Prelims are based off reading that is outside of the class. Tough grader and purely gives grade based on whether or not he knows who you are.

analysis.name: Kelly Pohland
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 11 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Kelly was a really great professor. She regularly takes the time to stop and check if we have questions about the material instead of just steaming ahead. She also gave us a short break in the middle of lecture to go over logistics like deadlines and tests. This helped us to stay organized and refresh us in the middle of class.

Overcomplicates simple calc 2 concepts. Homework also gets longer and complicated for no reason. She also isn't transparent about class mean and median which makes it difficult to gauge how you're doing. There are quizzes in every discussion so it's mandatory to attend.

If her class is open, then take it. She is an amazing lecturer. As a student who did not take BC in high school, she was very thorough in teaching new concepts. She is also very accessible during OHs.

Professor Pohland was really nice in this class was just sometimes unnecessarily difficult. Homework is pretty hard but doable. The discussion was not helpful because the TA was going through a worksheet really fast. Everything is graded very picky.
Lectures were alright. Would often spend way too much time answering one person's questions. Tests were hard, and they never gave practice representative of the actual test.

analysis.name: Sun Peidong
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 22 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: went into the class expecting to learn from her but every class is a presentation on the reading due that day from a student. already disappointed. on top of that, she interrupts student presentations so nitpick irrelevant things and go on tangents using metaphors that barely connects to the conversation. didnt learn anything from her. disappoined.

Worst history class I've ever taken. Expectations/criteria unclear and changed syllabus as late as March;as a result, grading is based on vibes. Graded on an interview+presentation; you aren't taught how to do any of these things. Expects high amount of background knowledge on subject matter. Very disorganized class, with no clear direction. Avoid.

She dumped the course load on students with zero guidance, then acted like giving actual feedback was beneath her. I went to office hours and somehow ended up in a therapy session about me, because she refused to talk about the class. Then she handed out grades like she was drawing numbers from a hat. And let's not forget the unprovoked yelling,

Add on: she will give you NO indication of grades, bullies students who are presenting, and bases grading on whether she likes you. I have an above 4.0 GPA and this class single-handedly ruined my prospects of summa cum laude. She does not teach whatsoever, instead relying on students to give presentations on articles written by other scholars.

If you like your GPA, avoid this class. Professor does not communicate grading standards at all, and your end of semester grade is seemingly random. She likes to bully students in class, and teaches nothing but expects students to already know everything. If you like Maoism, borrow a book from the library. Do not take this class.

analysis.name: Meng Qi
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 11 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: BS from Tsinghua; PhD from UC Berkeley. She's “nice,” but the flipped classroom feels like buz 💻, which is expected. No rubric, so assignments are unclear and feedback is vague (“minus 1 point for blah”). Individual and class assignments are tough; the curve spans all sections—think twice before sharing what's on the exam 🫵🫵🫵 no partial credit

Did not learn a single thing. Never taught in class, not once. Misinformed me about when the last day of class would be and made me miss a crucial assignment. Then when asked about it, her response was that she believed it didn't matter since I "wouldn't have done well on it anyway". None of my peers nor I took a single thing away from this class.

For how ever many supposed "degrees" she has, she actually might be one of the least competent profs I've ever met. She has zero common sense. She makes tests intentionally hard, tricky, & unfair. Just know that email-her and in-person-her are two completely separate personalities. Email version chooses violence. IRL version is sweet as pie.

Conniving. Her flipped classroom method is very ineffective. Class starts really easy and gets unimaginably complex very fast. She seems nice but don't be fooled, she's not. She doesn't know how to teach because she herself just finished school. Sections get treated differently, you'll see why. Just try to keep up and work with others

Flipped-classroom. You copy videos for homework, then do a harder version of the content in class. No explanation of the material.
