review_highlights.comment: Okay Professor.  Half of every lecture is history/politics and other stuff that isnt tested.  His tests are kinda hard but he tells you exactly what to study.  Plus he gives extra credit, so you can end up with a pretty decent grade.  I hear better things about him than Wissink.

I took this class last spring. I attended a total of 6 lectures and one section. I failed the midterm, and I am certain that I failed the final.  I managed an 80ish average on the homework, and ended up with a C+ in the class.  Schulze utilizes Kentucky Windage, and it WORKS.  I didn't learn a whole lot, but I got through it.

Schulze is the man. He kept lectures interesting and didn't require too much reading of the textbook. He does rant a bit, but go to section, do the MyEconLab problems, and try to do well enough to utilize Kentucky Windage and you're golden in his class.

Horrible professor, seemed disinterested in teaching and focused on research. Hard to get in touch with. His merits are that he's quite a character and humorous. Expect to be teaching yourself the course through takenote.
Taught us about love in econ terms and was interesting to listen. Prelim questions are all multiple choice and covered during lecture. Didn't do the readings but still understood everything.

analysis.name: Catherine Blumenkamp
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Iconic. A breath of fresh air at Cornell. Organized, thoughtful, clear, communicative and committed. No pretension, just dedication to student learning and a justice-oriented classroom. Bonus if you get to work with her in the CF+TC! Highly recommend - she goes above and beyond, shows up to all the student events to support, and is 110% engaged.

A very dedicated professor who is organized, thoughtful, and caring. She is genuinely concerned about learning and wants students to be engaged and uplifted. She has a calming and encouraging vibe. One of the best professors at Cornell - loved going to class every day.

If you don't like group projects or going to class outside of class time, beware. Thursdays are just people presenting group projects, and you need to do extra credit events (lectures, fashion exhibits, etc) on weekends or during non-class hours to do really well. She is forgiving and changes grades all the time. Nice, but a boring lecturer.

Fashion, aesthetics, and society is a fun and interesting class w a minimal workload. We had projects (one group, one individual), discussion posts, and an attendance grade. The prof is so sweet and gives ample time to complete assignments

analysis.name: Anne Blackburn
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 19 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment:
ASIAN 1118 FWS: Power, Protection, and Liberation: Buddhist Paths to Flourishing

Lectures were VERY dry, 1/2 class was falling asleep. But overall, EASY course to get an A in. You don't really need to do readings to excel, but having some background knowledge is helpful.
She gives clear feedback, is very kind & understanding + lenient

She was exceedingly boring and the only thing drier than the lectures was her personality.  I was so interested in the topic, she's just not a very engaging instructor.  She would be better off in a library somewhere than in a classroom.
What's wrong with taking a class to fulfill a requirement?  Does this mean she has to be nasty?  Does this mean I don't work hard?  I felt like she was more than a little unreasonable and moody.

Prof. Blackburn is very intelligent and nice, although a bit boring. I found her lectures to be slightly dry but dense with information, which to me suggests that any problems are my own. As far as grading, I thought she was good and fair (which doesn't mean giving an A for every student).

After the awful comments on here, I was worried.  But this is the easiest class I've ever taken here.  She is nice, and while her lectures are confusing and dull more often than not, the individual projects assigned are quite interesting and enlightening for your information.

analysis.name: Caitlin Blanchfield
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 3 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: The class was largely structured like a conversation related to the readings. The readings, however, are heavily biased so conversation is limited to a mediocre regurgitation of an agenda. Not only this, but the conversation had little to no direction so it felt stagnant and useless.
Incompetent lecturer. She brings very little information to the lecture other than just describing the image on the slide.
Her lectures are very disorganized and unprepared

analysis.name: Theo Black
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment:
If you want a professor who cares, Theo is your man. He brings incredible enthusiasm to the classroom and not only cares about you as a student but as a person.

I HIGHLY recommend taking any classes you can with him. There will be lots of reading, but the pay off is well worth your time, he always brings out the best in you!
incredible instructor. extremely caring. puts so much effort into teaching the class. amazing class too.

Professor Theo is extremely welcoming and encouraging, and especially supports the students who work to be better actors. His love of Shakespeare, Ecological and Social theater is contagious. And when he performs in class, he commands the whole room!
Amazing teach with great talent!! Honoured to have learned from him

analysis.name: Larry Blume
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 35 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: He is disoriented, disorganized. He takes wrong notes to class several times. He is good at making jokes and telling stories but not giving lectures and explaining things. He gets impatient when you ask silly questions. I would have done better if I started working by my own earlier.

Blume is a poor explainer of the concepts in class and then when the tests arrive they contain material you have never seen before. He neglects to inform the TA of anything that will be on tests so the review sessions are worthless at best. He hands out no syllabus and cites simpsons paradox when talking about grades (look it up).

Class is unorganized. There's barely any structure to the materials. He basically teaches whatever comes to his mind. No quiz answers were given and problem set solutions were not followable. The TA does all the work for the class and all he does is show up at lecture. The majority of the final was materials NOT discussed in previous assessments.

"Considering recording lectures for his superiors to see!"  Lol... like Easley?  He is very smart, arrogant, a pain in the ass to deal with, yet broadly interesting; he enjoys and has knowledge of lots of random things. He's not out to get you, he is just extremely disorganized and teaches as if you're having an intellectual chat over coffee.
if you're going to take game theory 3680, you might as well skip lectures and watch these http://www.academicearth.org/courses/game-theory

analysis.name: Blake Nguyen
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Dr. Nguyen is one of the most intelligent professors at Cornell. Though lectures are sometimes a little dry, he presents information in an easy to understand manner and is always available to answer questions after class. As the manager of the Teaching Dairy, Blake is one of the people at Cornell who really walks the walk and talks the talk.

Honestly my favorite professor at Cornell. I had him for ANSC 2500 and ANSC 3540 and its obvious he enjoys teaching/the material. The information is very straightforward & you don't need previous knowledge of dairy to do well. Just show up to class, pay attention and you'll do fine. Blake wants you to get tons of hands-on experience through labs.

Blake is one of the kindest professors in the Animal Science Department. He has a dry sense of humor and tries to make you laugh during lecture. He is very specific on his tests and wants you to use the terminology from class. Blake wants everyone to succeed in his class and labs are very fun!

A delightful professor, and a genuinely good person. He's got a dry sense of humor that he uses at unexpected times, which keeps things lively. He makes an effort to actually get to know the people in his class. His previous experience as a practicing vet makes his teaching that much better.

Pretty good professor. Really knowledgable about what he teaches. Probably one of the most knowledge in the dairy science field to be honest. Courses are not content heavy and easy. But grading is mediocre.

analysis.name: Kevin Bloomfield
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: The amount of readings assigned was an overload and I wasn't able to balance the rest of my classes with his class. He talks extremely pedantically and makes overly worded jokes that make no sense. Also the amount of essays assigned were a lot, it was like 4 to 6 essays per month each being minimum five pages with a ten page research paper later.

Professor Bloomfield ensured that material load throughout the semester was always at the right level and offered substantial feedback throughout essay writing process. An already interesting topic was reintroduced to students in more formal readings such as scholarly articles and primary documents on past climatic events. Good first year class.

Prof. Bloomfield assigned a lot of reading at first, but lessened it when told by students that it was too much. Essays were graded fairly, always gave good feedback and was available to help. Towards the end of the semester became lenient and allowed us to do whatever we wanted for the final project, such as a group project on a current event.
Excellent teacher, amazing person really wants you to do well.

analysis.name: Jim Blankenship
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 44 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Insanely tough. This class broke me, and I definitely bled a little... Exams waaay harder than practice prelims, and curve is competitive (if there is a curve). Each exam is a puzzel with all sorts of unexpected twists, memorizing provides tools to solve them. Be good at taking exams. If you can think beyond the objectives, you will go far kid.

Jim is a nice guy, but his actual tests were 5x harder than the practice tests(actual tests from previous years). It could also be that this course was completely online this semester due to COVID. During the semesters where this class is offered in-person, the class is usually fine. Stay clear of this class until in-person instruction resumes.

Makes mud as clear as water. Excellent professor! His review sessions are short and sweet. Material can seem a little heavy, but is actually not that bad, especially with all the tons of help he and his assistants offer. Always wandering the study center asking if students need help with material, and asks for feedback on quizzes and tests.

took this molecular cell bio. class during the summer during the 6 week session. if you can, take it! covering something as deep as cell bio in 6 weeks means at least 25+ hours of work per week for each of the 6 weeks, but if you keep up, you will be rewarded with a wealth of knowledge. Also, there were only 4 people in the class- amazing.

You will remember this class for your entire life. Excellent auto-tutorial course. The optional "review" sessions led by Jim are good refreshers for exams or before Unit tests. Workload=life consuming. Talk to TAs in the center and clear questions up ASAP. Jim is always in the center helping TAs with unit test, grading, tutoring.

analysis.name: George Boyer
analysis.verdict: Tough course, but students consistently rate the teaching highly, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 44 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Out of the professors so far, Boyer was the best. The test was fair and covered exactly what he said it would (2 short answer questions). Graded pretty generously too. He'd make jokes here and there in lectures which made class more lighthearted, would 100% take his class again. Slides were detailed, just skim the readings and you'll be fine.

Had him as the first professor for Work Labor Capital. Very passionate about the subject matter and had lectures memorized. Straightforward and fair test, provided a study guide, sample question, and breakdown of the final. Was the only professor in WLC to do this. Great sense of humor and enjoyable lectures. Would take again.

First module of ILRGL 2350. Boyer kept the class engaged and I found the material to be very interesting. Exam was fair and covered what he said it would. Readings covered the same material as lectures

The homie Boyer was really funny and got the main idea across in each of his lectures. Super entertaining and a genuinely nice guy. It was easy to get excited about the topic and then the test was straight forward. What else do ya want?

Boyer is a great guy and he keeps lectures interesting. Highly recommend taking this class! Material is interesting if you like history. Graded by 2 exams and optional paper only. Exams are straightforward if you prepare well. Review sessions before each exam and comprehensive slides posted. You don't really have to read or attend class.

analysis.name: Nicolas Bottan
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 3 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Awesome professor. Very knowledgable about his work and incredibly understanding to the different levels of comprehension his students have in statistics/economics. The course work is NOT difficult if you come to class, ask questions, and participate. I highly suggest you take his course if you are willing to participate as you will get an "A".

Very kind professor and engaging during lecture. He allowed problems sets to be worked on with a partner but they were still very hard and sometimes not covered in lecture. I would take another course by him though!
great professor, I really enjoyed his class!

analysis.name: Vanessa  Bohns
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 41 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Best Prof. I've had so far at Cornell. Awesome lectures, clear powerpoints and she really knows what she's talking about. Always available to help. She's young, excited about the topic and super helpful. Prelims are challenging, but fair if you study and attend lecture.

Professor Bohns is amazing!!!! She was my instructor for intro to OB and faculty advisor during my credit internship--she was very organized, very knowledgeable, very accessible, and clear on what she expects from her students. She also genuinely cares for the development and growth of her students. Spectacular professor!

LOVE this prof, her lectures are easy to understand but make sure u take notes on what she SAYS, discussion sections super helpful, READ and UNDERSTAND the case studies. They are very useful, especially for prelim. Prelim was fair and most people did rlly well.
I wish I could take this class again, Prof Bohns is def one of my fav profs here

Was truly one of the most unreasonable test administrators I have ever experienced in my entire life. She gave us lectures that had nothing at all to do with our prelim. Gave us no guidelines for the essay assignment that she had us do. She lied to us that a practice test she put up had nothing to do with the test, the test was the same questions.

Great Professor. Lectures are really interesting and explain the topics quite well. Need to do a lot of textbook reading outside of class. She also offers plenty of opportunities for extra credit and seems genuinely interested in your success. Moderatly difficult, but with hard work can earn a good grade.

analysis.name: Chris Bordoni
analysis.verdict: Tough course, but students consistently rate the teaching highly
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 19 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Without a doubt the best class I took while at Johnson. Unlike most other case-based classes, your weekly task is not to respond to/solve a prescribed set of questions. Rather, you're provided with an ambiguous situation that you're expected to navigate on your own and for which there is not one "right answer". An enriching and rewarding class.

One of the best classes (and professor) at Cornell Dyson and Johnson. The teaching style encourages participation (and includes cases), and the content is very applicable to the real world. Took his class before my consulting summer internship and was surprised how much I referenced his teaching (and frameworks) during the internship!

One of the few professors that makes you understand why A. you are paying $$$ for tuition and B. why he is up there, and you are down here. This is the only strategy course that truly exists at Cornell (don't kid yourself with the others). Avoiding this course due to its difficulty is doing you (and your parents who pay your tuition) a disservice.

This is probably one of the best classes I have taken in my 4 years here at Cornell. Pretty tough class but teaches you a lot about critical thinking skills and applications to business. Bordoni himself is very smart and make the class very interactive and teaches the class very well. Only critique is the grading is opaque but the class is amazing.

No grading criteria ever given. Class discussions and cases were amazing. Class filled with highly motivated students. TAs were pretty useless. Has a strange class policy about feedback, very non-specifc. Had a policy of class equity which was basically he wouldnt give anymore feedback then the basic ones on the case memos

analysis.name: Bob Turgeon
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 46 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Professor Turgeon seems to actually wants his students to fail. The exams test the smallest most ridiculous bits of information that may or may not have been mentioned in class, and you really have to understand how to apply the information taught in class for the tests. The TA's are great though and section can actually be enjoyable.

Nice and approachable professor who encourages questions; was also fast at responding to emails. Quizzes aren't hard per se, they just sometimes test you on more obscure concepts. While slightly boring, I found the lectures to be more helpful than the textbook. Discussion sections have attendance - not too helpful but they were interesting.
Don't take unless you'd like to fail. Your grade is comprised of 3 tests.

Unless it's a requirement, do NOT take this class. The lectures are incredibly boring, but tests draw heavily from random points from lecture. The class is poorly organized, and some TA's can't even answer your simple biology questions. Cornell, if you're reading this, please replace BIOG1140 with a better introductory Biology course.

Honestly a horrible teacher. I really tried in this class and he offered no help. I went to his office hours to reach out for help and he smiled at my struggle and tears. On top of this is lectures are irrelevant and incredibly boring. I would not recommend this class to anyone that I like.

analysis.name: Vicki Bogan
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 35 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Got the grade I wanted in the class but by far the worst professor I've ever had. Narrow-minded; doesn't have any degree of practitioner's view on the finance world; was not able to comfortably answer 75% of student's questions. Tests don't test what is "covered" in the course. You'll learn just as much by watching The Big Short and Too Big to Fail

Possibly the worst lecturer at Cornell. She reads directly from the slides and makes you buy textbooks for no reason.  Course is pretty much behavioral finance 101. Easy class but boringggggg....tests require you to memorize her slides.. would stay away unless you need finance req

She really doesn't know what she's talking about in Fixed Income.  The book is excellent however so taking this course should be treated as almost an independent study of sorts.  She does grade generously though and is a very nice lady.  If you're interested, take this class, teach the material to yourself, and get an easy A while your at it.

The most condescending professor I've ever seen at Cornell. Yes, she might look nice at first, but she just thought she's right although we caught some errors on her slides/solutions. Decided not to answer our questions and directed us to her TA but eventually turned out she's wrong. So condescending.

PPS304: Exams are multiple choice and short answer questions but there isn't enough practice for the short answer questions so you're basically left to fend for yourself. The problem sets just aren't a good reflection of what's tested on exams. Also leaves all the teaching to the TAs, who grade almost everything too. Get ready to memorize A LOT.

analysis.name: David Bonter
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Bonter is just the best. If you have just the slightest interest in birds and are an undergraduate, talk to him. He is supportive, caring, and very knowledgeable about research. Gives great constructive feedback. Class is easy, but teaches you so much. Don't slack off tho, or your grade can slip. Note class has long waitlist.
Took 2400. Great class. Dave is super inspiring and cares about students. I really like him. Friends of mine have had him as an advisor, and only good things on that front as well.
David Bonter is a fantastic professor and person who really cares about this students. If you're interested in ornithology, you should email him to get on the waitlist for his class. You'll love it.
Amazing professor. NTRES 2400 usually fills up very quickly and has a long waitlist, but if you're at all interested in birds, definitely take this course.
There is no one at Cornell that will care about you, help you, or teach you more than Dave did for me.

analysis.name: James Booth
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Kind person, & extremely intelligent. However: He went through slides way too fast, & I ended up teaching myself the whole class & R programming language. Provided very few practice problems you'd see on exam. Concepts were not really explained thoroughly. 8am+kinda boring lecturehalf an auditorium of kids passed out. Would not take again :/

I think that most of the unhappy reviews are because he taught intro stats (I can see him being difficult to learn from in intro stat). But for an advanced class he was good. Taught the theory in class and gave us examples of how to use R to actually apply the theory in the real world. Met with me 1 on 1 when I was behind and helped me catch up.

Very meh professor. He seemed like he had no desire to teach this class at all as his lectures were very boring with few examples of how to do the statistics for ourselves. Additionally he went awol right before the final and students were panicking because we didn't know what was going to be on the exam and the TAs couldn't help us either.

He is very knowledgeable and nice, but he gives his lectures either too fast or too slow, and tbh, kind of boring. Exam mean and median are lower than previous years. Part of the reason is due to his ambiguous lecturing style... Overall I learned a lot from this class, but I won't take his class again.

analysis.name: Brian Kirby
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 13 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Exams were unnecessarily difficult. The mock job interview was graded entirely on vibes, and it honestly feels like grades depend on whether he likes you. Lectures make no sense, he's impossible to contact, never replies on Ed, and posts assignments the morning they're due. Fluid Mechanical Dissection is fun, but expect last-minute instructions.

I couldn't really get a read on him. Sometimes he seemed to care, but other times he came off dismissive and a bit arrogant, which made it hard to ask questions. His grading felt pretty arbitrary, and he was often unresponsive and hard to reach

The smartest person I know. He wrote the whole textbook and his knowledge extends further. He learned every student in the class's name. He was a frequent at discussion sections and OH. If he sees students, he comes over to talk and will answer questions. Not great at responding to email. Class was chill because the whole course is in his book.

Great professor.  He writes on the blackboard in an extremely organized fashion, only erasing the oldest thing, and keeping everything in chronological order.  The class is hard, and the frequent random quizzes are a nuisance, but his lectures are some of the best I've seen.

Funny guy.  Young, into cool music and surfing, very personable fellow.  Also very helpful if you have a question, clears it up pretty easily.  Makes a lot of jokes that unfortunately a lot of humorless engineers are incapable of understanding.  Only complaint is the incredibly slow grading

analysis.name: Bryn Rosenfeld
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: I have to disagree with the excessively negative reviews. Her FWS is definitely challenging, but Professor Rosenfeld is a great person and great teacher who genuinely wants her students to succeed. Super approachable outside of class and really knowledgeable in her field.

Professor Rosenfeld's class was outstanding. She provided thoughtful, actionable feedback consistently and was very supportive. She teaches the material in a clear and engaging way. With her guidance, I increased my course engagement and contribution to class, and it led to my steady improvement in overall performance over time :))

It's been an honor taking Professor Rosenfeld's class on Russian politics. She is always happy to hear from students, is upbeat, critically assesses our knowledge of course content, and is super encouraging all around. There was A LOT of reading and memo writing to do for this seminar, but I still enjoyed this class all around.

I did not take a class with her, but I had her as my advisor. I don't know what others are on about, but she was probably one of the nicest people I have met at Cornell. I talked to her about my project ideas, and she was both interesting and kind.

I gained a lot from this class. However, I would not recommend taking a class with Professor Rosenfeld just for the sake of fulfilling a requirement, the class is much harder than the average FWS. If you want to improve your writing (and don't care about your GPA) I would strongly recommend taking a class with her.

analysis.name: Brad Ramshaw
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Professor Ramshaw is an amazing lecturer and did justice to my first experience with quantum mechanics. He organized the class really well and his lectures were on point.

It was a really hard class for me, but Professor Ramshaw was very helpful and accommodating through the entire semester. I scored around the median for every assignment and exam and still somehow ended with an A? I would strongly recommend this Professor though, super chill guy.

What a caring man. Caters and accommodates to all your needs and then some. Responds well to pranks, has watched Friends, just an overall God. You can ask the same question thrice and he'll patiently answer it in clear, detailed prose. I have nothing but the highest level of respect for this man.

analysis.name: Daniel Buckley
analysis.verdict: Tough course, but students consistently rate the teaching highly
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 10 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: In  my opinion he gives the best lectures in all of Cornell! Absolute gem! He teaches with a style that just gets to you and makes you want to learn more about microbiology.

One of the greatest professors! He made me a lot more interested in biology. He respects the students a lot and as well as every silly question a student might ask, whenever in class or office hours or emails. Additionally he always smiles at you!!! If he is going to teach another higher level class, I would definitely take whatever the subject is.
i loved professor buckley's lectures and he is very kind and helpful. i really enjoyed microbiology bc of him, his lectures were engaging and interesting
One of the best biology professors! Very inspiring.

Such a caring professor. He really wants his students to succeed and actually learn. Cornell should have more science professors like him. But, I did SO bad in the class because the exams were just ridiculously hard. But, I think the homework grades will save your grade.

analysis.name: Florentina Bunea
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 23 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: She really cares about her students and wants everyone to understand. Very approachable. I've heard this class in the spring was impossible but as an average student I thought this class was pretty easy. The tests were exactly what we did in class.

Mostly good. 95% of grade is three prelims, rest from an optional homework. You don't turn in the other homework for credit, but do them to do well in the class! Also do all the discussion problems. If you can do all those, then exams are not bad. But each exam is 5-6 questions. I messed up 2 questions on 2nd prelim and tanked my grade to an A-.

Ms. Bunea is pretty good at lecturing. The exam problem is very similar to the problems she goes through in lecture or the ones she gave in recitation. The class is easy as long as you pay attention in class. She also posts all the notes on canvas which is very helpful. Recommend taking this class with her!

She is an AMAZING professor who teaches clearly and explains the material well. I put in effort, did my work on time, and received a good grade. She has a slight accent and uses a blackboard, but overall she's an enthusiastic and kind woman. I was confused by the negative reviews I read, anyways this is a great class and professor. 100% recommend

She's been teaching this class for a long time so it seems like she has lost interest. Lectures are pretty boring and people usually stop showing up to class halfway through. She does post all the notes online though and the material itself isn't too difficult. The TAs are also not great. Make sure to review the discussion questions and do homework

analysis.name: Carlyn Buckler
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Other review was definitely not accurate. She picks favorites and then is rude to those who ask questions. Grading criteria is unclear, and she gives unclear answers about it. Very few assignments, which is nice, but not worth being held hostage for an hour because she won't let you leave if theres nothing left to do or you finished the assignment

- struggles with classroom technology to the point of wasting class time

- struggles to present coherent lectures, most are a disjointed direct reading of the slides

- never prepared for class

- lacks any clear expectations for assignments, presented various several year old rubrics and struggled to provide any clarification
Do not take this course. Do yourself a favor and take regular oral comm.

Good communications class, especially for E&S/plant science/agsci majors. Assignments included: mock newspaper article, video, elevator talk, and final presentation. Carlyn was a riot (affectionate), and I thought the assignments were reasonably graded. Only con was that she was disorganized at times & expectations weren't always straightforward.

analysis.name: Diane Burton
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 9 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: The class wasn't so bad (besides being way too much group work for my liking) but Burton was the worst lecturer I've had at Cornell. Unprepared for class, talks in circles, doesn't make any sense, etc. I wanted to like her, since she's super enthusiastic, but she was just not good at teaching.

This professor's employment makes the school look like a joke. The most disorganized and unfair professor I've ever encountered. Our entire grade was based on a singular research paper that was given to us 2 weeks before class ended, without any instructions and a misleading rubric. The presentation we worked on for 3 months had no weight. Fire her

As a management class, this has been one of the worst-managed and least-organized classes I have ever taken at Cornell. It's great that she's so passionate about the subject matter, but vague lectures, poorly-defined grading criteria and a lack of awareness of how much work she assigns are massive detriments to her teaching style. Bad website, too.

Professor Burton is very enthusiastic about students but she speaks VERY fast and to be honest, she's not a very good teacher. She's hardly clear and very difficult to follow. Her lecture slides are also absolutely horrible. Its literally a sentence and a bunch of pictures and is impossible to comprehend after lectures. I wouldn't take her again.

While Professor Burton is lovely, ILRID 1700 was among the least organized of my Cornell career. There is a ton of weight on group work, making it almost impossible to succeed if you get a bad group (speaking from experience) and lectures are generally all over the place. She obviously cares a lot about the material but the class was a real pain.

analysis.name: Nathan Bulthuis
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 7 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: really nice and easygoing, contract grading system so you decide what grade you want and do the amount of work for the grade you want. no technology in class or slides only writes on the whiteboard so hard to focus on what is said a lot of the time

Dr. Bulthuis is an exceptional professor & explains complicated content in a clear, palatable way. He makes you want to learn the material. This course was on the Philosophy of Aquinas--heavy duty stuff--& Dr. B he made it enjoyable and exciting. I was blown away at this course from beginning to end. He wants you to get it & is very approachable.

Bulthuis explains course concepts very clearly and in an engaging way. Even when readings initially seemed incomprehensible, Bulthuis managed to make the material seem simple. I was always excited to go to class (even though it was in the morning!). I am hoping to major in philosophy because I loved his class so much. He is my favorite professor.

Bulthuis did a good job of explaining the concepts in each unit. There was a decent amount of reading (some weeks easier than others, watch out for Hume) and our TA gave us the option of in-class quizzes or responses (500 words) based on the reading. Exams were fine if you did the reading and studied well. Papers were tedious but fairly easy.

I went to office hours to catch up on classes I missed, and Professor Bulthuis helped me to understand things about philosophy I didn't even know I didn't know. He is the reason I want to be a Philosophy major, after taking his INTRO class. His lectures are phenomenal and engaging. His grading is tough but undeniably fair.

analysis.name: Christopher Byrne
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Regarding his film class at DU. He refused all criticism and brought up his Playboy career, calling Hugh Hefner a “good guy.” Then he'd show films mocking lesbians. He obsessed over AI tools, pushing students to subscribe to ChatGPT and Grammarly Premium instead of actually teaching writing.

At some points, Prof. Byrne shows you what he can do as a professor but at others has you questioning why he's there. He's laid back but seems to teach the course because he's interested in random topics. Not because he necessarily cares about student growth. Unfortunately, don't take it unless you plan on going into media or have to.

Prof. Byrne is one of the best comm profs. He is knowledgeable and genuinely a good person. He gives good feedback to improve on assignments, and is generally reasonable with grades and has clear rubrics. Due dates are fairly spaced out, and overall he's just a great person and professor
Professor Byrne is the GOAT. I loved his Sports COMM class and I think any student interested in sports would too! The topics are very interesting, and it was a pleasure to attend class.
Such a caring and compassionate professor who is passionate about his work. Has fantastic energy!

analysis.name: Judith Byfield
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 8 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Interesting, but a LOT of material. Readings tedious at times, I ended up skimming a lot. Lectures LONG; prof mostly lectured nonstop, reading from her notes for 75 mins while students struggled to keep up with notes; no visual aids such as powerpoints. Discussions the best part of the class; fair grader. I'd give the class a B-

Stay away from this woman at all costs. Don't risk your grade whatsoever. If she doesn't like you it's over for you and your grade. Has no structure to her class and is honestly the worst professor I have ever taken at Cornell. Listen to this please. AVOID.

Professor Byfield knows her stuff! She knows a lot about the things she teaches. She  is always willing to help students out if they need extensions on papers etc. She is an engaging teacher and encourages discussion during discussion sections, otherwise she lectures in her other classes (still interesting).

This professor is a menace. Her grading was completely unclear and she obviously gave students bad grades for no other reason than she didn't like them. Taking this course was like watching a child on a power trip every day. stay away

Oh God please, do yourself a favor and stay away. No clear grading rubric. Impossible to please. A class where hard work doesnt pay off. Unreasonable amount of reading. Long, boring lectures. Dont, just dont.

analysis.name: Byoung Hwang
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 8 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Hes a super nice and helpful guy, and a great lecturer to top it off. There is a good amount of self learning for this course but it is not too challenging. The stock trading game and portfolio projects are fun and easy. Lectures give you career insight as well as basic finance knowledge 10/10 professor
The lectures are super clear. He is a really good lecturer, super nice, and is always available for help. The class overall is easy. Little reading and the tests are easy.

Professor Hwang has been one of my favorite professors this semester and it's a shame I didn't get to take his class in person. He explains the material very well and in a way that makes it fun. Sets clear expectations and clearly cares about his students. 2240 is an easy class but I'd recommend it even if it wasn't because Prof. Hwang is great.

Prof. Hwang is there best in the business. Whether your into Fi or not, you will need to thoroughly understand the fundamentals of equities and fixed income if your going to be big in this game. Hwang is a world class Corporate Finance prof, and renown researcher in Behavioral-Fi. World class prof, in a world class program. Sit up front.

Literally one of the best professors at Cornell. When a classroom's desk space was too small for an in-person prelim during COVID, he immediately moved the exam online. His lectures were engaging, and some of the few recorded ones that I looked forward to watching. Honestly the best.

analysis.name: Sahara Byrne
analysis.verdict: Highly rated with a manageable workload
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 18 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Sahara Byrne is the best professor I've had at Cornell so far hands down. Super nice and caring about her students, and a great teacher. Take 2200 if you can.

Professor Byrne truly cares about her students and not just their success inside of her class, but their outside involvement as well. I was fortunate to have her for COMM 1101 in person this year and she was so understanding about all the struggles of online learning for the kids who were not on campus and pushed back assignment dates to help.

Best professor and best class I took in my 4 years at Cornell. Prof Byrne respects you as a student and person, and if you're willing to work hard and put in an honest effort, you will learn more in her class than any multiple choice/test based class. Amazing professor, even better person!

Byrne's is awesome, I love her! That said, don't take the class for granted. Make an effort and THOROUGHLY read the assigned chapters in the book and pay attention. It will pay off in the end. Byrne's is such an exciting prof, and I'll miss not having her next semester. Great Personality!

Great teacher. Engaging, interesting and pertinent lectures. Creates great study guides that is incredibly clear what will be on the exam. In office hours, very helpful and fun to talk to. Projects were fun and final paper was not exceedingly difficult.

analysis.name: Byron Suber
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: This class was definitely not what I expected.  DANCE 232 (Modern Dance Technique II) was really more yoga than dance.  Sometimes we rolled around on the floor or did strange acting exercises.  If you're free-spirited and hippie-ish, you will probably enjoy the class.  If you're looking for something similar to a jazz class, this is not it.

This class can be as challenging as you want it to be, depending on how perfect you want to get with all of the exercises. They're not intricate, but they are useful for developping good form, etc. Prof has some interesting thoughts on ballet- it's not a traditional ballet class at all, but can be a great experience no matter what your preferences.

In Byron Suber's ballet class you will get an amazing work out, spiced up with tidbits of dance history. He makes you reevaluate the Ballet aesthetic and think about its socio-cultural implications. His style is based on classical ballet, however, be prepared for unusual combinations that will challenge you to ditch your habits.
really bright, really funny, really motivated, and really ****. just the way i like my dance teachers.

analysis.name: Marina Caillaud
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 29 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: I honestly expected an easer class kind f like other non major bio major classes but i am really glad i took the class because it was super interesting
Great professor and great class. My friends at other elite universities do not have classes on bees, and they wish they had
I can't believe how much I learnt in this class and it was really fun
Funny and passionate. Her exams are NOT easy and very specific.
She's a great professor! Very friendly and very fair. If you have a genuine problem with the course you can bring it up to her and she'll probably agree with you and fix it!

analysis.name: Bruce Monger
analysis.verdict: Highly rated with a manageable workload, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 104 reviews, this is a reliable picture

review_highlights.comment: Professor Monger is one of the most passionate professors I've had at Cornell; I've heard many stories of students who've taken his class and afterwards claim he changed their lives. Easy class with some memorization. Pay attention in class (or watch them online) & study lecture slides and you'll do really well. Great science for non-science people

One thing I loved about this professor is that he actually cares about what he is teaching. He urges students to care about the environment and instills a will to make a positive change in the world. I am not a science major but this class broadened my perspective on the world greatly. I struggled with some of the memorization but not too hard.

I did not really enjoy this lecture. I'm not a science person in general, so I was not really looking forward to this class. People call this class an easy A, but it is a lot of memorizing. I'm not good at memorizing, so I did not do as well as I had hoped. However, it is pretty easy for a science class, and you get extra credit for attendance.

Took this class in Fall 2024. Prof. Monger is a great lecturer, and the exams are pretty straightforward, though very memorization-based. They're also based on the lecture slides, which tends to have a lot of information, but he does upload lecture recordings. It's not a purely low-effort class, but it's manageable to do well in it overall.

Lecture were a bit boring, that being I usually reviewed slides afterward if I wasn't following along. Tests sometimes required memorization of certain statistics but questions are relatively straightforward and some are repeated from past prelims. I took the lab (BIOEE 1560) and I would recommend even for nonsci majors, it was a grade boost.

analysis.name: Carl Franck
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: A difficult class made extremely frustrating by the professor. Unable to end a sentence with the same thought he started on, hearing him talk actively hurt my understanding of circuits. He lectured by coming in and happily rambling until we got up and left. A nice guy, and grading was okay in the end, but anything I learned was from the lab manual.

He's really nice and means well, but he can't teach. Grading scheme is unclear, tests don't always reflect the content, & lectures aren't helpful in any way. His strategy is to look at the slides he took from another teacher for the first time in class, which means he has no idea what he's doing and sometimes gets his own iClicker questions wrong.
Carl is really nice, but he's an awful teacher. Basically didn't teach or spell out his expectations for anything (data analysis techniques, experimental methods, report writing...).
Carl is really helpful and cares about his students (ie. meets up with individual students to discuss labs and writing reports). However, he's not particularly clear at times.
Very confusing recitation.  It would help if this instructor learned to talk more slowly, and not jump so quickly from topic to topic.

analysis.name: Caroline Levine
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Professor Levine is passionate about both the course material and its application towards advocacy and political change. Her lectures are very interactive, and she grades based on in-class writing, out-of-class essays, and participation.
Attendance and interaction are 40% of the grade, and there is a lot of reading before each lecture. The assignments are not difficult. The content is interesting for a humanities course.

I took ENG 3795 Communicating Climate Change, and I genuinely believe it was the most important class I took in Fall 22'. Caroline is personable, lenient, and a good lecturer, and I feel that I came out of the class as a much better climate communicator. There are many essays and readings, but IMO they are all worth your time- 9/10 would take again

Very easy 2-credit class taken for a GPA boost. Just 2 papers and a final paper. Attendance required unfortunately. Her lectures are so awfully dry. It was an easy A but probably not the most worthwhile class I could take.

analysis.name: Linda Canina
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 18 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Super funny and caring professor, very willing to help even outside of class. Her homework is harder than her actual prelims, and sometimes you wonder in the middle of the exams how the questions can be so simple. Just go to class, do the homework, and do the most basic studying, and you can get at least an A-. Lots of partial credit in exams.
I learned mostly on my own from doing her homework that lined up well with the exams. Her lectures were...interesting to say the least. Beware the final was harder than the prelims.

Horrible professor. Her class is completely unorganized. You feel like the class content just get thrown at you from no where. There is no slides available, everything is just talked about and written down. Even the readings, she just says to read them once in class, and then would cold call random student and expect you have done everything.
Very Harsh grader. The lectures were very tedious and not overly helpful. Even the most studious will find success in this class to be challenging. I would avoid.

Canina is perhaps one of the best professors at Cornell! She cares deeply for your understanding of the principles and will take time to go over the material before pre-lims. Speak with her early, let her get to know you and she will help you along. Also very involved in the entrepreneurship community at Cornell. AMAZING PROF!

analysis.name: Stephen Ceci
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 6 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: The course was enjoyable and Ceci gives thought-provoking lectures. The books are actually interesting, unlike some FWS classes. There's extra credit opps and the 6 essays are peer-graded, so getting an A is totally possible! The 3-hour lectures do get a bit tedious. The class was a bit disorganized (esp. paper deadlines and PollEv quizzing).

This class was by far one of the most interesting classes I have ever taken. Papers are peer graded and the median grade is the grade you get. There are 6 papers, 6 discussion sessions, and 12 quizzes + lectures. 6 extra credit points are offered. The lectures are quite long but are usually very interesting. Take this class!

Nice guy, but posted wrong essay prompt to Canvas. When students pointed out the mistake, they said to answer the prompt on Canvas. 4 days before the due date, they changed their minds and said to write a different prompt. This was after many students had already completed and submitted their essays. Never taken a class that was so mismanaged.

Six Pretty Good Books was co-taught by Prof. Ceci and Prof. Macy (and has been taught by Prof. Hancock before). I took it to fulfill requirements, but it turned out to be a REALLY interesting class. Authors included Duncan Watts and Malcolm Gladwell, and some visitedskyped in for Q&A. Definitely talk to Prof. Ceci after class--he is SO nice!!
Amazing lecturer, caring, simply one of the best professors Cornell has to offer. Take this class!

analysis.name: Alfred Center
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 9 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: He's a nice guy.  He really cares about his students.  He helps you get a job after graduation if you haven't found one.  He looks mean, but he's really a teddy bear.
Professor AI is a great person. He truly cares about his student, and has a kind heart for grades. Would like to take his class again.
Lectures are useless. And he's grading is really arbitrary. At the end of the semester a lot of people had issues with the grade.
Very helpful once you prove you know what you're talking about, very intimidating otherwise.
Most practical classes at this university.3838

analysis.name: Glenn Case
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 29 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Very poorly organized and failed to properly support his students. Office hours were incredibly full, to the point where it could take hours to get a single question answered. Answer keys for discussions and practice exams didn't exist. Heard him use a mocking voice to answer a student who asked a question he thought was easy to answer.

Phys 2207 has one of the most awful course structure. I paid to learn and they give you a online resources instead of teaching in class and call is "flipped class." Also they don't provide answers to practice exams. Which does not make any sense since they are meant for "practice," but how can I practice without knowing if I am doing things right.

Lectures and demonstrations were clear and engaging. I used to dislike physics in high school but I enjoyed his class. The content can be hard to process, but there is also a lot of opportunities to attend office hours or ask questions after class. Gave me a solid foundation in physics.

Very eccentric guy, makes a lot of mistakes working out problems during lecture. Gives vague answers to students' questions in class. Extremely poorly written tests and no answer guys given for everything but homeworks.

Very clear and useful lectures. Tests are challenging. There are questions relating to labs and very specific concepts, which are given large point values. If you forget one of many concepts, you will lose significant points on the exam.  There is no curve, but a fair grading scale (class average will be a B). Prelims are optional with him.

analysis.name: Cecilia Earls
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 9 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: She's a nice person, just not a great teacher. Her explanations aren't always what the student asked about. Grading is 90% 3 prelims and 10% homework and you can have a one-page formula sheet on the exams.

Her lectures are the worst. She just reads off powerpoint so fast, you have no clue which things you need to jot down (if she even stays on that slide for that long), literally my second semester with her and I only understood the first 3 or so lectures of each semester and the rest is just trying to understand her cluttered powerpoint. NoBook4Help
Homework problems are nothing like the examples done, over and over again, in class.
The lectures are really long and tedious, going over very simple topics for a very long time. However, the homework is nothing like the lectures and are almost impossible.

She is not clear, and even some youtube videos make clear points than she did. She basically just read through slides. Morning class!! Attendance is not mandatory but there is a weekly quiz on Thursdays. Appropriate homework load. Not many practice problems. Use R to do homework but didn't require to code in exam. Allow one self-made formula sheet

analysis.name: Flaminia Cervesi-Mccobb
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 15 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Flaminia is great; she keeps class lighthearted and fun, but also really helps to jumpstart your Italian.  She knows how to work with students at all levels and is a genuinely nice person.  She takes our work seriously but is always good for a laugh during class.  If you don't slack off, you'll really enjoy and get a lot out of this course
Just because she's cute and from Italy doesn't mean that she's a good professor.  Picks favorites, pretty damn condescending, not much of an intellectual.

I've had two classes with her and she's wonderful! Can be tough at times but is seriously dedicated to helping you learn the material and is often quite funny. You obviously have to participate a lot since its a language class so I don't suggest taking it early in the morning or you'll be too tired to want to do the exercises

Flaminia is amazing, and I've had 3 classes with her...She is so patient, beyond helpful, and has no problem giving As if you pay attention and  keep up with the work. She also has a great sense of humor and seems to really care about her students.
Flaminia always seems bored in class.  She doesn't seem very smart, but that is probably because she hates her job. She can also take out this frustration by making fun of students in class.

analysis.name: James Cecil
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 8 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Cecil is challenging but at least he?s doing his job. Unconditional praise is not my idea of education. I?ve seen the stuff my classmates write in peer reviews, and it?s dribble, disrespect for a guy trying to actually teach something. You want the praise that you feel the ?Ivy League? warrants you? Take the time to write a halfway decent essay.

He shows up late to almost every class. But more importantly, he is perhaps one of the hardest graders at cornell. I am a great writer, and have always done well in english courses, but in his class, i struggled more than i  ever have in my life. His grading was incredibly harsh and unfair (at one point the class average was a C-).

Dude hasn't taught here for a year...but in light of the below comments, wanted to say one of my better classes. Make an effort, talk to him, and you're fine. He gives a damn and a lot of people end up with crappy grades cause they can't deal with having something asked of them..

For some reason, does not give out many A's. Teaches a very hard class, but ENGL 1108 has a high average grade, becasue the other TA's are easier. do not take this calss if you want an easy writing seminar at all - you will do a lot more and harder work than you have to.

great guy. intimidating first couple of weeks but warms up, everyone in the class ended up loving him.  knows his stuff.  put effort in and you'll do well.  shows up a little late sometimes.  more based on fiction than poetry.  great class!

analysis.name: Kuei-Chiu Chen
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews. Student experiences vary quite a bit, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 7 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Two faced. Classes were close to useless. It was all up to the students to cover the work. She ditched lectures very often and handed us to the oblivious TA's. Very moody as well.

One of the worst courses I've taken in Cornell partly because of the lecturers and partly because the tediousness of lab. Chen conducts the class with professionalism and with an iron fist. She is very strict but don't be afraid because she's like that with everyone. As a lecturer, she was monotonous. Wished she  made the course more interesting.

Professor Chen is extremely dedicated to teaching. Her lectures are well prepared and she will spend a lot of time during office hours helping you out if you need it. She is a very tough grader, but there are 400 students in the whole course, so she needs to be fairly strict. Overall, she is a very intelligent and dedicated asset at Cornell.

Learned a lot in lab and became more confident in my research skills, but she is too strict, especially on those research papers. Good lecturer but course is too time consuming, especially for a measly two credits!
She's pretty helpful, especially during office hours. Although quizzes/tests/papers were fair, she could be a tough grader at times. She really wants to help you learn, and it shows in her teaching.

analysis.name: Chris De Sa
analysis.verdict: Good teaching but expect to put in the work, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 14 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Genuinely the GOAT of hardware accelerated ML. His lectures are so tuff and often go into the math derivations behind a specific method i.e. he proved the decent lemma from the top of his head AND he can write torch code in real time (wow). A very nice guy during OH too, willing to work through examples together for clarification which is awesome.

He is very good at lecturing and clearly explaining concepts. However, CS 4787 has many assignments; I believe he managed to squeeze in 6 programming assignments, 6 problem sets, 5 paper readings, and 2 exams into a 15 week semester.

Most concepts taught in this course are very very useful. If you want to pursue your career as an ML/DL engineer/researcher, this course is basically mandatory, although many concepts are indeed difficult to understand.

De Sa was excellent in Principles of Large Scale Machine Learning. His lectures were clear, organized (notes on website), and very rigorous. I came out of the class feeling like I had strong skills in supervised learning, and good intuition for any related to ML or optimization. Material is used in practice in and is cutting edge for a ugrad course

De Sa was super nice, helpful at his OH, and gave awesome print outs of his lecture notes. The programming assignments (in python) build off each other so sometimes your just copy/pasting code and didn't take too long to complete. Overall I learned ML much better than CS4780 and used many of the concepts at my AI/ML internship, highly recommend.

analysis.name: Charlie Green
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: By far the best professor I had my freshman year. I learned a lot about writing as well as broadly disability studies. Would and 100% will be taking again.
Amazing professor, incredibly engaging and very welcoming to students. Would 100% take another class with Dr. Green.

This was such a good FWS to take my first semester of Cornell. Dr. Green cares very much about both the content of the course and his students. He also takes feedback and makes changes to the course based on that. Grading was very clear and based on effort and improvement over the course. He also offered extra assignments for extra credit. 10/10

Dr. Green was such a nice and encouraging professor. Course demanded a decent amount of work, but grades are labor-based and not quality-based. Aka your grade is an actual reflection of how much effort you put in. Chose interesting books and he tried really hard to make the material interesting. Participation matters. Would recommend!

analysis.name: Jacob Chestnut
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 19 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: A word that can be used to describe Professor Chestnut is actually in his own last name; he is a nut. Although his raw intelligence is high, his emotional intelligence is quite low (poor people skills). He often spends a good portion of class boasting about himself and is extremely unapproachable (unless you plan to glaze him in your interaction).

As others said, extremely pretentious and has incredibly low capacity to understand other's perception of himself–it's consensus that no one really likes him. He will blabber on and on about himself and his accolades for as long as time allows. 

Otherwise, very easy class. I stopped attending lectures very early on for aforementioned reasons.

Professor Chestnut definitely has some personality to him. Due to the "flipped" structure of the class, you essentially teach yourself everything, but thankfully the content isn't too hard. He clearly has some narcissism/ego problems, as he boasts about himself in almost every class, but realistically he's fine as a teacher.

This is the easiest stats class you can take. No R coding that other stats classes do and fair exams. There is only 1 prelim and a final, with the prelim average being 90%. The professor has a HUGE ego and overshares everything. The class is really disorganized and no one knew what was going on sometimes. Also it took 1 month to grade our prelim.

Honestly one of the worst professors I have ever had. He yells at students and sent my friend a three-paragraph nasty email asking why she didn't read his 120-page syllabus when she asked a question about the final. He talks about himself in every class, and you have to teach yourself the entire course. I don't understand how people like him.

analysis.name: Derek Chang
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 32 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Took a FWS with Prof Chang last semester. He is so adorable and he really cares about his students; this is great especially when COVID brings about many difficulties. Attention: A lot of hard history readings were assigned. They might be too abstruse for a stem guy like me.

Prof Chang is a super super nice guy, and you can tell that he really cares about his students. He's always willing to accommodate extensions and responds very quickly to emails. The only issue I had with the class is that we literally didn't get a single grade until our final grade, but I knew that coming in b/c of reviews on here lol.

Took a FWS with Professor Chang last semester. I gained so much from the in-class discussions & readings. Very caring and hilarious. My only issue is that we didn't get much feedback on our papers, so I don't feel like my writing skills improved that much. However, he told us that his main goal is for us to learn; he wants to give everyone A's.

Prof Chang is a great guy who knows a lot about this subject area and is very passionate about it. He just wants you to learn and enjoy doing so, and is more interested in fostering genuine interest and discussion than making you work really hard for a grade. This class is very manageable and not hard to do well in if you do the few things he asks.

Funny, engaging, and lenient. Originally there were four essays, but he gave everyone an A for one of them. Would have given a 5 if he were a little more punctual; he took long to upload discussion prompts and never returned our papers (I only got the final grade). Great course overall, and great for law & society minor. Would recommend!!

analysis.name: Cindy Kao
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 3 reviews so far, take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Professor Kao's lectures present upcoming design technologies, focusing on STEM. They are inspiring to students exploring the boundaries of design. Grades are easy, and the vibe is quite comfy. You learn to actually design and improve through the "learn by doing" style of this class. She brings a new face to design that I learned to appreciate.

So knowledgeable in the subject area, and clearly cares about her work. Class time is split evenly btwn lecture and in-person workshops, and there are weekly readings and Canvas posts. Two "big" Projects but you are given ample time and support to complete. Very tech saavy, smart, witty, and understanding. Will definitely take her again!
Such an understanding and engaging prof, workload is minimal and fun. Take this class :)

analysis.name: Cindy Hazan
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 60 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Not the easy A anymore that everyone claims it to be. The material is interesting but the structure is really strict and doesn't have many opportunities for improving grades. Even though the exams are online, the responses need to be super specific and the feedback from graders are unclear. I've taken many HD courses and this was the hardest one.

NOT EASY A! Lectures are boring, graded by TAs who don't want to talk to you,& it doesn't matter if you know the content; The exams are like "explain in 1 sentence," then take off points for not expanding enough. ppl like her bc she gives a FEW ppl free therapy,& it's cool for ppl who have never taken a psych class and don't know basic attachment.

Interesting course content, but you're solely graded on three exams that are worth a lot and are graded very harshly with points deducted for not including a single word despite them being online. There are also not many chances to improve your grade, and you receive very little feedback. Fun course material, but no longer an easy A anymore.

I was a TA for this course so I'm biased when I say TAKE IT!! It's so much fun to bond with your friends who might not be psych/HD majors because it's truly a class for everyone. Open-note prelims and relaxing afternoon lectures in Bailey Hall make for a great class to take in your last semesters at Cornell when you can get a spot.

Content wise, Human Bonding was a spectacular class and you can sense the years of experience/research she has in her lectures. But take *really* detailed notes! It's begun to live up to its 3000 level difficulty with some rather harsh grading.

analysis.name: John Cisne
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews. Worth checking if this fits your learning style, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 31 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: Even if you absolutely need a one-credit course to bring you up above the minimum number of credits to remain in good academic standing, do NOT take this class! I was pretty interesting in the material before coming to class, but Cisne just lulls you to sleep. And if you think this class is an easy A, don't take it...it's not. Studied for days! = C

The subject matter is actually pretty difficult but I got an A+ despite failing a bunch of quizzes because the curve is skewed.  If you sign up for the 9am lecture, bring a cup of coffee.  It's actually pretty interesting for an intro science class (although if you want an easy science class that's more fun take ntres201).

Hilarious guy, especially when he's not trying to be. I'm a bio major and I thought the class sounded interesting. For the most part, it's not. I really don't care about rocks. End of the class is actually about organisms, and kind of interesting. I wouldn't really recommend the class, but it wasn't that bad either.

If you just want an "A" and don't care about learning, take this class.  But if you actually want to learn and don't want to waste your time and money, don't sign up. There is no learning involved in this class whatsoever.  Lectures are completely disorganized.  Labs make no sense at all. I actually had been interested in geology, but now I'm not.

A very easy course that liberal arts majors terrified of hard sciences go to for a requirement. Weekly HW takes 40min max(go to office hours), quiz answer keys given beforehand on blackboard, T/F Final, and the lowest 1/3 of all grades are dropped! Take it if the topic interests you, otherwise you will zone out. A free A/A+ for engineers/scientists

analysis.name: Cliff Kraft
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time. Look into alternatives if you can
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 28 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: When I saw I had to take this course for my major, I looked at rate my professor and saw his low score..."It can't be that bad, right?" I said to myself... boy was I wrong... Never have I ever met a man who rambles on and on about material that HAS NEVER BEEN ON THE EXAM. his co lecturer ginger gives us actual information thats on the prelim.

He hates modern technology and progressive ideology, like a primitive ancestor resenting the first human who began to walk upright. Given that he was a Cornell undergrad fifty years ago, it makes me wonder how rigid the campus culture once was.

This class was so pointless. While some topics were interesting, Kraft does not have the ability to get his points across, which means that on the tests (which change in their format every other week, it felt like), everything you knew was solely based on readings or what you could manage to pick up from lectures (which no one showed up to).

Cliff Kraft gives the most incomprehensible and pointless lectures ever. You'd think a topic we spent 3 classes discussing would be on the prelim, right? Nope! The exam questions are often unfair and are based around concepts or vocab terms that were not extensively covered. The class is pretty easy but its such a rage bait.

Cliff Kraft Survival Guide: Try to find and appreciate his passion. His lectures are directionless but have purpose/connection in his head. You won't fully understand or appreciate why he's exploring a certain topic, and you can count on a tiny vocab word being the whole of an exam question.

analysis.name: Cindy Van Es
analysis.verdict: Well liked with reasonable difficulty, and ratings have been declining
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 89 reviews, fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: Probably the goofiest, nicest, & most likable professor I've had. It's difficult to learn the material as she teaches it, but the course is arranged so that you're able to master it before the exams. Lectures got a bit monotonous near the end of the semester, but her personality is always refreshing. Her course packet is insanely helpful. AWESOME!!

The sections, homework and lectures all reinforced concepts that we needed to know, and the exams were very fair and based on these assignments. Participation is recommended because a % of your grade is derived from your participation in random in-class activities. The sections are helpful, but the lectures are made extremely amusing by Cindy.

Cindy was a great professor, one of the best so far since being here at Cornell! She is willing to work with students if they have any issues, etc. Lectures can be engaging and brain breaks are the best! The TAs for the class were also extremely helpful and understanding. GO TO OFFICE HOURS! Cindy and the TAs are both amazing when you need help.

Cindy is a very kind person and a great professor! Its clear that she cares about us, and she structures the course/discussion sections in a way that gives students nonstop preparation for exams. Exams were not too bad after reviewing HW and section material, and there was extra credit on most of them. Only downside is lectures weren't recorded.

Was extremely unhelpful during Fall 2021 semester. With everything going on, Cindy showed disregard at the concern of her students. I understand that AEM2100 is a large class, but with omicron on campus, she did little to nothing to accommodate. Instead, she implemented a draconian zoom exam many other classes showed understanding with a take home.

analysis.name: Claudia Verhoeven
analysis.verdict: Tough course, but students consistently rate the teaching highly, but ratings have been improving recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 18 reviews, decent sample but could shift with more data

review_highlights.comment: I was in HIST 1690, which she co-taught with Professor Schmitt. Professor Verhoeven was genuinely amazing, had such wonderfully fascinating lectures and was obviously very intelligent. She is such an amazing lecturer and really immerses students into the culture and time period through examinations of art or events. One of the best professors ever.

She worked really well with Professor Friedland for this class. She gave good feedback, but it wasn't really applicable in this class (there was one exam, one paper, and one video project so you couldn't apply the feedback). Attendance not required but show up-- you could get away with not studying if you just pay attention and do the readings

Clearly cares a lot about the students, the course material, and making it accessible, engaging, and applicable. It pays off in a great course!
